body:
Three years ago I was translating contracts in Cairo.

Today I run marketing automation for a B2B company in Barcelona.

The plot twist wasn't the language. It was the day Python clicked.

I kept getting handed marketing problems that needed a developer's brain. So I borrowed one — line by line, prompt by prompt. n8n. Then Clay. Then Claude.

Now I'm posting daily for a year. Stack, process, lessons — all public.

Building in the open because the stuff I figured out the hard way shouldn't take anyone else two years.

If you're in marketing and you're scared of the technical side — read along. I'll show you what I broke first.

#MarketingAutomation #GTM #BuildInPublic #CareerJourney

topic: The full B2B GTM stack we run
body:
Our full B2B GTM stack — every tool, what it actually does:

↳ HubSpot — system of record. Deals, contacts, pipelines. Sacred.

↳ Apollo — sourcing. ICP filters first, enrichment later.

↳ Clay — research brain. Two tables: research, action.

↳ n8n — the wiring. 11 flows, 0 Zapier subscriptions.

↳ Claude — writer + researcher. Wired into n8n via API.

↳ Slack — operations channel for every triggered alert.

What's missing: a CDP. Don't need one yet.

Counterintuitive bit: most leverage came from n8n, not Clay. The flows that connect tools matter more than the tools.

What's in your stack? Curious which tool you'd rip out tomorrow.

#GTM #MarketingAutomation #ClayHQ #n8n #RevOps

topic: The 4 layers of any B2B Clay table
body:
Save this if you build anything in Clay.

The 4 layers any B2B table needs:

Layer 1 — IDENTIFY

Name, company, role, location. The minimum to know we have a person.

Layer 2 — ENRICH

Firmographics, tech stack, funding, headcount. Trap: enriching everything because you can. Enrich on demand, not by default.

Layer 3 — SCORE

How relevant is this row, today? Single column, 0-100. Built from Layer 2 + ICP rules.

Layer 4 — ACT

What happens next? Send to sequence. Push to HubSpot. Alert in Slack. One column = one decision.

If a Clay table doesn't have all four layers, it's storage, not a system.

Most tables I see are heavy on Layer 2, missing Layer 3 and 4 entirely.

Diagram in the comments.

#ClayHQ #RevOps #Frameworks #MarketingOps

*'**t need more leads*

Hot take from someone in the trenches:

Most B2B teams don't need more leads.

They need ten of the right ones.

The temptation in modern outbound is to scale the list. With Clay you can build a 50,000-row table by Friday.

Resist it.

Build the 800-row table instead. The one where every row has been read, scored, understood.

Add the column nobody else is adding — the trigger event, the team change, the regulation deadline, the funding stage. Specific to your ICP.

Every wasted email tells the right buyer you don't understand them.

The volume game in B2B outbound is over.

The depth game is the only one left worth playing.

Change my mind in the comments.

#B2BMarketing #Outbound #GTM

I work in Arabic, Spanish, and English every day.

Same product. Same value prop. Same target.

Three completely different shapes.

A campaign that converted 8% in English flopped at 1.2% in Spanish last month. Same hook. Same offer. Direct translation.

Why? Because the English line was urgency-led: 'ship this by Q3 or fall behind.'

Spanish B2B buyers don't respond to that. They respond to certainty: 'companies like yours are already running this. Here's the proof.'

Arabic readers want context first — the why before the what.

Most translation tools translate words. The job is to translate intent.

If your B2B marketing crosses borders and you're using DeepL to ship campaigns — you're leaking pipeline.

Run the message through a human who lives in the language. Or at least through Claude with a 'rewrite for cultural fit' instruction.

Cheapest A/B test you'll ever run.

#Multilingual #B2BMarketing #MarketingOps

Built this in n8n last week. Saving us ~12 minutes per inbound lead.

The flow:

1. Webhook trigger — HubSpot fires when a form fills

2. Function node — extract domain, drop free providers

3. HTTP node → Clay API — pull company size, tech stack, last funding

4. HTTP node → Claude — summarise the company in 2 lines + flag if ICP

5. Update HubSpot contact + post to #ops Slack channel if hot

Total nodes: 7. Total time per lead: 11 seconds.

What it replaced: an SDR doing it by hand for 12 minutes.

What broke first: Clay's rate limit. Added a 2-second delay node, fixed.

What I'd do differently: pipe the Claude summary directly into the Slack alert — saves another click.

This works for any B2B inbound flow with a CRM, an enrichment source, and an LLM. Pattern over tool.

Happy to share the JSON — drop a comment.

#BuildInPublic #MarketingOps #GTM

AI-written outbound is dead.

AI-researched outbound is just getting started.

The first wave: 'Use ChatGPT to write 1,000 cold emails.' Reply rates collapsed. Of course they did. Everyone got the same five templates.

The second wave is different.

Use AI to read.

Read the prospect's last earnings call. Read their job postings. Read the LinkedIn comments their VP left two months ago.

Then write the email yourself. One. Specific. To them.

We've moved our AI spend from generation to research. Reply rate moved from 4% to 11%.

The volume game in outbound is over.

The depth game is the only one left worth playing.

If you're still spinning up 'personalised at scale' sequences in 2026, you're losing to the operator who sends 30 emails a day with their own hands.

#Outbound #AI #B2BMarketing #RevOps

Most Clay tables I see are graveyards. 30 columns. 12,000 rows. Nobody's looked at them in a month.

Here's how ours is structured:

→ Identifiers (4): name, email, company, LinkedIn URL

→ Firmographics (3): size, country, industry code

→ Signal layer (5): hiring, funding, leadership change, mentioned tech, recent news

→ The action column (1): what's the next step for this row, today?

13 columns. Not 30.

The action column is the test. If you can't say what to do with a row, every other column is decoration.

Common mistake: building enrichments because you can. Pulling Clearbit data on a 12k row table for €0.02 each because Clay makes it cheap. Then doing nothing with it.

Rule of thumb: every column should pay for itself in a workflow within two weeks. If it doesn't, delete it.

What columns do you keep that you've never used?

#ClayHQ #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: What I learned my first week as a marketer
body:
What I learned my first week as a marketing automation specialist that I wish someone had told me:

1. Nobody trusts the CRM. The reports lie. The numbers lie. Everyone has their own spreadsheet. Earn trust by being the person who makes the CRM tell the truth.

2. The best people on the team aren't in marketing. They're in sales, customer success, and engineering. Make friends with them in week one.

3. Every 'simple' request takes 4x longer than estimated. 'Just send a quick email blast' = 3 days of work if done right.

4. The tools you inherit are usually 60% wasted spend. Audit before you onboard new ones.

5. Your job is to make other people look good. Not yourself. Counterintuitive but it compounds.

6. If you can't read an API doc by month 3, your career ceiling drops fast.

7. Your manager doesn't know what they want until they see what they don't want. Ship rough drafts early.

I made all 7 of these mistakes in my first three months. Save the post if you're earlier in your career than that.

#CareerJourney #MarketingOps #LessonsLearned

Five questions I ask before I touch n8n, Zapier, or any automation tool.

Save this — it'll save you a graveyard of broken flows.

1. Is this process repeatable enough to deserve code?

If you do it 5 times a week — yes. Once a month — probably not. Build the muscle of saying no to tiny automations.

2. What does it cost when it breaks silently?

Automations fail. The question is: what's the worst that happens before someone notices? If the answer is 'we lose pipeline for a week,' you need monitoring.

3. Who owns it when I'm not here?

Every flow needs a documented owner. We use a 'one-page README' inside the n8n flow itself.

4. Is there a simpler version that's 80% as good?

Often the answer is yes. A Slack reminder beats a 9-node flow.

5. Will this still make sense in 12 months?

If the underlying tool is brittle (e.g. depends on a HubSpot list ID someone might rename), it'll break and you won't know why.

Skip these and you build a graveyard of half-working flows nobody trusts.

Save it. Run it before your next 'wouldn't it be great if we automated...'

#MarketingAutomation #Frameworks #RevOps

Stack reveal series — part 1 of 4. The HubSpot side.

Most teams treat HubSpot as a contact database. We treat it as the system of record — and almost nothing else lives there directly.

What lives in HubSpot:

→ Companies, contacts, deals (clean, deduped, source-attributed)

→ A small number of properties — 14 on contact, 9 on company

→ Pipeline stages with strict entry/exit criteria

→ Owner assignment rules

What does NOT live in HubSpot:

→ Enrichment data (Clay handles that)

→ Outbound sequencing (Apollo + n8n)

→ Scoring logic (computed in Clay, written back as a single field)

Why: every tool is good at one thing. HubSpot's job is to be reliable, not clever.

The most useful HubSpot view we have is a single dashboard called 'pipeline truth' — nothing fancy, just deals × stage × age. Most teams over-build their CRM and end up trusting it less.

Tomorrow: Wednesday's build-in-public spec.

#HubSpot #RevOps #MarketingOps

*'**s flow*

Building this in n8n today, shipping Friday. Sharing the spec before I touch a node — feedback welcome.

Problem:

Inbound demo requests sit in HubSpot for 14-72 hours before someone reaches out. We're losing intent.

Goal:

Every demo request gets a Slack alert in <60 seconds, with a one-paragraph briefing on the company, written by Claude.

Plan:

→ Webhook from HubSpot form submit

→ Filter — drop personal emails and obvious junk

→ Clay HTTP — pull company website + LinkedIn

→ Claude HTTP — produce briefing in our voice

→ Slack post to #demo-inbound, owner tagged

Risks:

→ Claude over-summarising and missing the actual ask in the form (mitigation: include the form's free-text field in the prompt)

→ Clay rate limits at peak hours (mitigation: queue + retry)

If you've built something similar, what did I miss?

#n8n #BuildInPublic #ClayHQ

topic: Clay will be in every B2B stack within 18 months
body:
Hot take.

Clay will be in every B2B marketing stack within 18 months.

Three reasons.

1. The data layer of GTM is moving from CRM to a separate place.

Your CRM is for truth. Your enrichment + scoring + research layer needs to be flexible. That's a different shape of tool. Clay won this category.

2. The cost of compute keeps falling.

What costs €0.05 per enrichment today will cost €0.005 in 2027. The economics of running 50,000-row tables go from 'maybe' to 'why wouldn't you'.

3. AI agents need a substrate.

Every agent demo I've seen looks great until you ask 'where does the data live and how does it update?' Clay tables are the answer most operators arrive at.

Counter-argument I'm watching for: a CRM-native version of this gets built and bundled (HubSpot, Salesforce). Possible but unlikely in 18 months — they don't ship that fast.

Disagree? I want to hear it.

#ClayHQ #RevOps #B2BMarketing #GTMStrategy

Shipped the demo-alert flow this morning. Closing the loop on Wednesday's spec.

What works:

→ Slack alert lands in 41 seconds (target was 60)

→ Briefing covers what the company does + likely use case in 3 lines

→ Owner tagged in Slack — first reach-out happening 30 min after submit, down from 14+ hours

What broke:

→ Claude over-flattered the prospect on the first three runs ('forward-thinking pioneer in...'). Added an explicit 'no marketing language, just facts' instruction. Fixed.

→ Two false positives from competitor sign-ups. Added a competitor-domain filter.

Total time built: 3.5 hours. Total time saved per week (estimated): 7-9 hours of SDR triage.

Reply-time correlates with close rate. The teams responding fastest to inbound win disproportionately.

JSON in the comments for anyone who wants the skeleton.

#n8n #BuildInPublic #RevOps #MarketingAutomation

Stack reveal — part 2 of 4. The Clay side.

We run two main Clay tables. That's it. The discipline of two tables changed everything.

Table 1 — RESEARCH

→ ICP candidates we're investigating

→ Heavy enrichment: tech stack, hiring, leadership change, signals

→ Refreshed weekly

→ ~3,400 rows

→ Output: a confidence score and a 'next move' column

Table 2 — ACTION

→ Only contacts confidence-scored above 70 from research get promoted here

→ Light columns: contact info + sequence stage + last touch

→ Refreshed daily

→ ~640 rows

→ Output: pushed into Apollo sequences and HubSpot deals

Old way: one giant table. Slow, expensive, full of stale rows. Nobody trusted it.

New way: separation of concerns. Research is patient and rich. Action is fast and lean.

If you remember one thing: split your Clay into research and action. Tomorrow's framework post explains why.

#ClayHQ #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: First six months in a new country
body:
Nobody tells you this about moving countries to take a job:

The first six months are about translation, not work.

Translation of how meetings actually happen.

Translation of what 'urgent' means here vs back home.

Translation of who has authority in a room when nobody says.

Translation of when 'maybe' means yes and when it means no.

I moved from Cairo to Barcelona to do marketing automation. Day one I could write a campaign brief in three languages. Day one I had no idea how Catalan industrial culture handled disagreement.

The gap between language fluency and cultural fluency is enormous. And it's where most of your performance review lives.

What helped:

→ One person who'd done the move before me, willing to translate the unspoken stuff

→ Saying 'I might be misreading this — can you walk me through it?' often

→ Accepting that month 1 was for listening, not building

If you're hiring someone from another country: give them six months to translate before you measure them.

If you're making the move: give yourself the same.

#CareerJourney #Multilingual #Leadership

The single most useful Clay framework I've found:

Split your tables into RESEARCH and ACTION.

Research table:

→ Rich. Slow. Patient.

→ Heavy enrichments, web scraping, signal columns

→ Updated weekly or on schedule

→ Cost: high per row, but on a small number of rows

→ Job: figure out who's worth talking to

Action table:

→ Lean. Fast. Strict.

→ Contact info, sequence state, owner — that's it

→ Updated daily, sometimes hourly

→ Cost: low per row, runs constantly

→ Job: move the right people through a sequence

Three rules:

1. Rows only flow research → action, never the other way

2. Anything that's an enrichment lives in research, never in action

3. Action table never exceeds 1,500 rows. If it does, your filter from research is too loose

Why this works: research and action have different update frequencies and different costs. Mixing them in one table is why most Clay setups stall.

Save this if it helps. Diagram in the comments.

#ClayHQ #Frameworks #RevOps

topic: Why most B2B segmentation is broken
body:
Most B2B segmentation is broken.

Typical setup:

→ Industry (manufacturing, software, finance...)

→ Company size (SMB / mid-market / enterprise)

→ Geography (NA / EMEA / APAC)

→ Maybe a 'tech stack' tag if you're fancy

Same campaign goes to all of them. Same hook. Same offer.

What's missing: the segmentation that actually predicts buying behaviour.

→ Trigger event (new VP, funding, regulation deadline)

→ Pain stage (not aware → aware → searching → comparing → deciding)

→ Decision shape (single buyer vs committee, top-down vs bottom-up)

→ Budget season (most B2B has predictable annual spend cycles)

The fix isn't more data. It's smarter columns.

Score each prospect on those four dimensions. Run different campaigns at each combination. Reply rates climb.

Took us 6 months to figure this out. Sharing so it takes you less.

#B2BMarketing #ABM #GTMStrategy

We run 11 production n8n flows today. Quick tour:

→ Inbound demo alerter (Slack briefing in <60s)

Why n8n, not Zapier:

1. Self-hosted, no per-task pricing — we run thousands of executions/day

2. Native HTTP, function nodes, code blocks — flexibility ceiling is much higher

3. Version control through Git — flows are real artefacts

Cost: €5/month VPS. Time saved: ~25 hours/week across the team.

Tomorrow: build-in-public — what I'm rebuilding next.

#n8n #MarketingAutomation #RevOps

Rebuilding one of our oldest n8n flows this week.

Current flow:

Every night, Claude rewrites the summary field on every open deal in HubSpot, based on the last 30 days of activity.

What works: AEs love it. Saves them rereading 2 weeks of notes before a call.

What doesn't:

→ Runs on EVERY open deal, including dead ones

→ Costs us ~€40/month in Claude tokens for summaries nobody opens

→ Tone drifts month to month — sometimes punchy, sometimes formal

Rebuild plan:

→ Filter: only deals with activity in the last 14 days

→ Style sample: pin a 200-word example summary, instruct Claude to match

→ Output: one 'what's the next conversation about' field, separate from the summary itself

Estimated savings: ~70% token cost, better consistency, more useful field.

Shipping Friday. Will post the receipt either way.

#BuildInPublic #n8n #HubSpot

The MQL is dead. The B2B teams still chasing them are competing for last place.

What an MQL was supposed to mean:

'A lead that has shown enough interest to be passed to sales.'

What it actually means in 2026:

'Someone who downloaded a PDF, attended a webinar, or hit a 47-point lead score threshold based on website visits.'

Why it broke:

→ Buyers do 80% of their research anonymously now

→ Webinar attendance correlates negatively with intent in some segments

→ Lead scoring models are gamed by buyers, vendors, and competitors

→ AEs ignore MQLs because most of them are noise

What replaces it:

→ Account-level intent signals (multiple people from same company researching)

→ Trigger-based outbound (funding, leadership change, tech adoption)

→ Direct AE-led prospecting on a small, well-researched list

→ Inbound qualification via real conversation, not form-fills

If your marketing team is still optimising for MQL volume, you're optimising the wrong number.

Optimise for AE-accepted leads. The number that survives sales reality.

#B2BMarketing #DemandGen #RevOps

We ran the same campaign in English, Spanish, and Arabic last quarter.

Same target persona. Same product. Same offer.

Reply rates:

→ English (UK + Nordics): 6.8%

→ Spanish (Iberia + LATAM): 1.4%

→ Arabic (MENA): 9.1%

Same content. Five-fold gap.

What we found when we dug in:

The English version was urgency-led: 'Your competitors are already running this. Don't get left behind.' Nordic + UK buyers responded.

Translated to Spanish, urgency reads as pushy and presumptuous. We rewrote it to lead with social proof and certainty: 'Empresas como la tuya ya están operando esto. Aquí está cómo.' Reply rate climbed to 7.2%.

Arabic worked first time because the opening was already context-first: explaining the WHY before the offer. That cultural pattern matched.

Translation tools translate words.

Operators translate intent.

If your B2B is multilingual, please don't run it through DeepL and call it done.

#Multilingual #B2BMarketing #Outbound

Stack reveal — part 4 of 4. The Claude side.

Where Claude sits in our stack today:

→ Inbound briefings (1-paragraph summaries on demo requests)

→ Outbound research (reads a prospect's last 5 LinkedIn posts before I write)

→ Deal summary fields in HubSpot (overnight)

→ Form copy variants (3 versions per landing page)

→ Translation pass on multilingual campaigns (after a human does the first cut)

→ Internal knowledge: pulls from our Notion to answer GTM team questions in Slack

What I've learned running Claude in production for ~14 months:

1. The system prompt does most of the work. We have ~200 lines of standing instructions per use case. Spend the time.

2. Pin examples. 2-3 'this is what good looks like' examples beat any amount of abstract instruction.

3. Don't hide the model. Tell users when they're reading Claude output. Trust collapses fast otherwise.

4. Separate generation from research. The model is much better at reading and summarising than at inventing.

Cost: ~€220/month across all use cases. Time saved: 30+ hours/week of human work.

#Claude #AI #MarketingAutomation #RevOps

topic: The job that taught me to write
body:
Before marketing, I translated contracts.

Boring work. Repetitive. Sometimes 12-hour days reading the same legal phrases in three languages.

It taught me three things that turned out to be the most useful skills in marketing:

1. Sentence structure matters more than vocabulary.

A clear short sentence in the buyer's language beats a clever long one. Always.

2. Intent is harder to translate than words.

'Best regards' in English ≠ 'saludos' in Spanish ≠ 'تحياتي' in Arabic. They all signal different relationships. Marketing is the same — translating intent across personas, not just channels.

3. Repetition is a teacher.

Doing the same task 10,000 times reveals patterns no theory class will. Marketing has the same property: write 100 emails before you optimise. The pattern emerges.

My career path looked random for a while. Translator → marketer → automation specialist. From inside, every role taught the next one.

If your background looks 'unrelated' — list what it actually taught you. Half the things you think aren't transferable, are.

#CareerJourney #Multilingual #LessonsLearned

Before you ship a B2B campaign, the 5-question diagnostic.

Save it. Run every campaign through it.

Q1 — WHO is this for?

Not 'mid-market manufacturers'. ONE persona you can name. Title, daily pain, internal incentive, what they Google at 11pm.

Q2 — WHAT do you want them to think after consuming this?

Not 'be impressed by us'. Specific: 'these people understand my problem better than I do'.

Q3 — WHAT'S the only-you angle?

If a competitor could publish the same content, the content isn't doing the job. Find the angle ONLY you can credibly take.

Q4 — WHERE will it land?

LinkedIn vs cold email vs landing page vs sales conversation. Each demands different shape.

Q5 — HOW will you know it worked?

ONE metric. Not five. Define it before launch, measure it 14 days after.

If you can't pass all 5 in 90 seconds, the brief isn't ready.

Half the time this sends me back to rewrite. The discipline pays.

#B2BMarketing #Frameworks #ContentMarketing

topic: Most automation content online is theatre
body:
Most automation content online is either theatre or homework.

Theatre: cherry-picked screenshots, 'look at my beautiful flow', everything works first time, no broken bits visible.

Homework: tutorial content built for a perfect example case, never tested at the messy edge of production.

Almost nobody shows the messy version.

What I'll show this month:

→ The actual problem (boring, often)

→ The spec on Tuesday — including the bits I'm not sure about

→ The build on Friday — including what broke and what I had to redo

→ The cost (real money) and the time saved (real numbers)

Why this matters: most people fail at automation because they copy demos, not production. Demos hide the failure modes.

If you're tempted to automate something next week, pick a real problem and build it alongside me. Comments are open the whole month.

Friday's first flow: rebuilding our ICP scoring engine.

#BuildInPublic #n8n #MarketingAutomation

Flow #1 of 4. Shipped today.

Rebuilt our ICP scoring engine from a Clay-only scoring column to a Claude-augmented n8n flow.

Why rebuild:

→ Old score was 5 numerical inputs, weighted. Predictable. Too rigid.

→ Missed 'soft' signals — recent leadership changes, partnership announcements, hiring patterns

→ AEs trusted it less and less, was becoming decoration

New flow (8 nodes):

1. Trigger: Clay row update

2. Function: pull company + last 30 days of public signals

3. HTTP → Claude with explicit rubric ('rate 0-100 on these 4 dimensions')

4. Function: weight + combine into single score + write back to Clay

5. Conditional: if score >70, push to action table

6-8. Notification + logging + alert if Claude returns malformed output

Cost: ~€0.014 per row × ~3,400 rows weekly = ~€48/month.

AE-reported usefulness went from 4/10 to 8/10 in week 1.

This pattern (numerical rubric + LLM grader) works for scoring almost anything in B2B GTM. Pattern over tool.

Spec for next week's flow drops Tuesday.

#n8n #ClayHQ #Claude #BuildInPublic

Flow #2 of the July series. Spec today, ship Friday.

Problem:

Cold deals in HubSpot don't auto-resurrect. Sometimes a buyer goes quiet for 4 months, then their company announces something we should react to.

Right now we have no system for that. AEs miss it. Sometimes a deal walks away because we were silent at the wrong moment.

Goal:

Detect public signals on companies with paused or stalled deals, and surface them to the deal owner the same day.

Plan:

→ Daily cron — pull all HubSpot deals in 'paused' or 'stalled' stages

→ For each: look up company in Clay, fetch last 7 days of news/funding/hiring signals

→ Claude: judge whether any signal materially changes our angle

→ If yes: Slack DM the deal owner with a 2-line briefing + suggested next move

Risks:

→ Noise. Most days, most signals are not actionable. Mitigation: Claude's first job is to filter, not summarise.

→ Missed signals. We're trusting Clay's news endpoint. If they miss it, we miss it.

Friday: receipt.

#BuildInPublic #n8n #ClayHQ

The most useful skill I have, nobody taught me in school, in any of three languages.

Reading API documentation.

Sounds like a developer skill. It isn't.

Once you can read an API doc, you can:

→ Wire any tool to any other tool

→ Stop waiting for engineering to build that integration you need

→ Understand what's actually possible with the software you're paying for

→ Build a marketing system that does what your team needs, not what the tool's UI happens to expose

Most marketing roles still hire for experience inside specific tools. HubSpot, Marketo, Pardot.

The roles paying double are looking for people who can read a Clay or n8n or Claude doc and wire something useful before the end of the day.

If you're a marketer who hasn't read an API reference yet — start with the simplest tool you know. Pick one endpoint. Make a single curl request that does something real.

Once it clicks, your career trajectory bends.

Took me about 8 weekends to get past the fear. Best 8 weekends I've spent.

#CareerJourney #MarketingAutomation #MarketingOps

topic: 3-layer mental model for any data table
body:
Three layers I think through before I add a column to ANY data table — Clay, HubSpot, Notion, Airtable, doesn't matter.

LAYER 1 — IDENTITY

What does this column tell me about the row's existence?

Examples: company name, domain, country, headcount band.

Test: would I refuse to act without it? If yes, it's identity.

LAYER 2 — SIGNAL

What does this column tell me about the row's relevance, today?

Examples: recent funding, hiring, leadership change, mentioned tech.

Test: does this change month-to-month? If yes, it's signal — and it needs a refresh schedule.

LAYER 3 — ACTION

What does this column tell me about what to do next?

Examples: ICP score, sequence stage, owner, last touch date.

Test: would I write to a sequencing tool from this column? If yes, it's action.

The mistake almost everyone makes: mixing layers. Identity columns that update weekly. Signal columns that never refresh. Action columns calculated by formulas nobody can read.

Tag the columns. Refresh accordingly. The table tells you what it's for.

Save this. Diagram in the comments.

#Frameworks #RevOps #MarketingOps #ClayHQ

Day 2, Claude returned actionable=true on 38 deals. Investigated — the prompt was reading 'minor news' as actionable. Tightened the rubric. Down to ~3-4 per day now.

Most teams running Apollo and Clay together do the handoff badly.

Here's how ours works.

The wrong way:

→ Apollo is the source of truth. Anything you find in Apollo gets shipped to a sequence.

→ Clay is bolted on for enrichment, scattered, inconsistent.

→ HubSpot fills up with low-quality contacts.

Our way:

→ Apollo finds candidates only. Filtered by ICP.

→ Candidates are pushed to a Clay 'research' table — never directly to a sequence.

→ Clay enriches, scores, decides who graduates to the action table.

→ Only graduates get pushed back to Apollo for sequencing.

Result: Apollo holds maybe 4-5% of what we found. The other 95% sat in Clay and either got promoted later or didn't.

Reply rate: 11.2% on contacts that go through this pipeline.

Industry average for cold B2B: under 3%.

The handoff is where most teams leak. Don't let your sourcing tool be the gate.

#ClayHQ #Outbound #RevOps

Flow #3 of the July series. Tuesday's post described the handoff. Today: cleaning up where it breaks in practice.

Problem:

Apollo sometimes pushes contacts that are duplicates, ex-employees, or in the wrong country. Cleanup eats 2 hours a week of someone's time.

Plan:

→ n8n watches Apollo's output webhook

→ Function node: deduplication check against existing Clay rows (by email + company)

→ Function node: company-country sanity check

→ HTTP → Claude: 'is this person likely still at this company based on their LinkedIn last activity?' (only fires if uncertainty flag is set)

→ If clean: write to Clay. If flagged: write to a quarantine table for human review.

Edge cases I'm worried about:

→ Multinationals where the country guess is genuinely ambiguous

→ False quarantines piling up if review never happens

Mitigation for #2: weekly reminder Slack ping if quarantine is non-empty.

topic: B2B sales is a two-decision problem
body:
Most B2B sales conversations are framed as one decision: 'should we buy this?'

They're actually two decisions:

1. Is this problem worth solving now?

2. Are these the people to solve it with?

Reps lose deals all the time at #1, thinking they lost at #2.

How to tell the difference:

→ If the buyer asks about pricing, timing, contracts — you're winning #2 but losing #1. They believe in you, not the urgency.

→ If the buyer asks about case studies, references, alternatives — you're winning #1 but losing #2. They believe in the problem, not yet in you.

Different problems. Different fixes.

If they don't believe in the urgency, more references won't help. You need to widen the cost-of-inaction conversation.

If they don't believe in you yet, more urgency won't help. You need proof.

Most B2B 'objection handling' content treats every stall as the same. It isn't.

Diagnose before you respond.

#B2BSales #SalesProcess #GTM

Flow #3 shipped. Apollo → Clay cleanup is live.

Receipts from week 1:

→ 1,180 contacts processed

→ 84 flagged and quarantined

→ 26 of those were duplicates

→ 41 were country-mismatched

→ 17 were Claude-flagged 'probably no longer at company' (10 of which were correct)

Time saved: ~90 minutes/week on cleanup.

Cost: €6/month in Claude calls.

What broke:

Day 1 we quarantined 200 contacts because the country normalisation function didn't handle 'United States of America' vs 'USA' vs 'US'. Embarrassing. Fixed.

What's next:

Flow #4 — meta-flow. A monitor that watches every other n8n flow we run and alerts if anything looks anomalous. Spec Tuesday, ship Friday — last of the July series.

#n8n #BuildInPublic #ClayHQ

Sharing one of our outbound prompts that actually works. Anonymised but real.

The prompt (system-level, simplified):

You are writing a cold email from Saleh, a B2B marketing operator. Style: short, specific, no marketing language. Open with one observation about the prospect's company. Ask one question that costs them <90 seconds to answer. Maximum 75 words. No 'I hope this finds you well'. Avoid 'just wanted to'.

Per-email input:

→ Their last 3 LinkedIn posts (truncated)

→ Their company's last news mention

→ Their job posting (if hiring)

→ A trigger event specific to their situation

Three rules I add to every prompt:

1. If you don't have enough data to be specific, say so — don't invent.

2. Refuse to write if the prospect's role doesn't match the ICP given.

3. Output the email AND a one-line 'why this should work' justification, separately.

Rule #3 is the killer. Because Claude has to justify the angle, the email gets sharper.

Hand-edit time per email: ~90 seconds. Reply rate on this stream: 13.4%.

Steal the structure, not the wording.

#Outbound #Claude #AI #B2BMarketing

Two years into marketing automation. I still feel like an imposter every Monday.

Posting this because most career advice tells you imposter syndrome 'fades with experience'.

Mine hasn't. I've just learned to work with it.

What changed:

→ I stopped trying to feel competent and started trying to BE useful. Different metric.

→ I stopped comparing myself to senior creators with 80k followers. I compare myself to me, six months ago.

→ I stopped treating 'I don't know how to do this' as a confession. It's just data. The next move is finding out.

→ I stopped pretending in meetings. 'Walk me through it slowly' has saved my career more than any technical skill.

Imposter syndrome doesn't go away. The relationship with it changes.

If you're 6 months into a new role and feel underwater — you're probably not actually drowning. You're just learning faster than your confidence is updating.

Save this if you needed it today.

#CareerJourney #MentalHealth #ImposterSyndrome

topic: Outbound diagnostic — data, copy, or targeting
body:
60-second diagnostic for outbound that isn't working.

Save this. Run through it next time your outbound stalls.

Reply rate under 2%? You have ONE of three problems.

1. DATA problem

Symptom: high bounce rate (>4%), low open rate (<35%).

Cause: stale contact data, wrong people on the list.

Fix: enrichment refresh + ICP filter tightening, before you touch copy.

2. COPY problem

Symptom: opens are fine (>40%) but replies are silent.

Cause: the email reads like every other email they got this week.

Fix: rewrite with one specific observation per email. Cut sentences. Cut your name. Cut the CTA boilerplate.

3. TARGETING problem

Symptom: opens fine, replies happen, but they're 'wrong person, please redirect'.

Cause: persona is misaligned to who actually buys.

Fix: walk through your last 5 closed-won deals. Who did the first call? That's the persona.

The mistake everyone makes: assuming it's a copy problem because copy is the most fun to fix.

Diagnose before you rewrite.

#Outbound #Frameworks #RevOps

Reframe I'm coming back to lately:

Most B2B teams sell features.

The companies that win sell what the features unlock.

When you sell features, conversations are about:

→ Spec sheets

→ Pricing per seat

→ Integration depth

→ Roadmap promises

All real. All important. All commodity-able once you have a competitor.

When you sell outcomes, conversations change:

→ The buyer becomes a leader, not just a user

→ The deal size grows because we're solving an organisational problem

→ The reference customer is a peer who already cleared the goal

→ The competitor isn't another vendor — it's the cost of inaction

Same product. Different sale.

The reframe is uncomfortable internally — engineers and product managers prefer to talk about the thing they built.

But buyers buy outcomes. Always have.

If your sales conversations sound like spec comparisons — that's the gap.

#B2BSales #B2BMarketing #GTMStrategy

Q3 GTM Playbook — post 1 of 20.

Our full B2B outbound architecture, top-to-bottom.

SOURCING — Apollo (filtered to ICP + region only)

↓

RESEARCH TABLE — Clay (~3,400 rows, weekly enrichment)

↓

SCORING — Claude via n8n (rubric-based, written back to Clay)

↓

ACTION TABLE — Clay (~640 rows, contacts >70 score)

↓

SEQUENCING — Apollo (4-step, manual gate at step 3)

↓

REPLIES — HubSpot deal created on positive reply

↓

BACKFEED — Closed-won outcomes update the ICP rubric monthly

Three things most teams miss:

→ The action table never goes above 1,500 rows. Quality, not quantity.

→ Manual gate at step 3 — a human reads every email at deeper stages.

→ Backfeed loop — closed-won data improves scoring monthly.

PM today: 5 Claude prompts that close.

#GTM #Outbound #ClayHQ #RevOps

Q3 Playbook 2/20 — five Claude prompts in production right now.

1. The OPENER PROMPT

Reads prospect's last 3 LinkedIn posts → outputs ONE specific observation. Hard rule: refuses if no specific signal exists.

2. The BRIEFING PROMPT

Triggered on demo request. Outputs: company in 2 lines, likely use case, 'red flag' if any. Never marketing language.

3. The OBJECTION-PRE PROMPT

Pre-call: takes the deal's last 3 emails + LinkedIn → predicts top 2 objections + one-sentence rebuttals.

4. The MULTILINGUAL PROMPT

Takes English source + target language + 'cultural fit' instruction. Outputs not translation but cultural rewrite.

5. The DEAL-SUMMARY PROMPT

Overnight job. Reads last 14 days of activity per active deal → 'next conversation is about X' field.

All five travel. Steal the structure, change the inputs to fit your ICP.

#Claude #AI #Outbound #B2BMarketing

Q3 Playbook 3/20 — the multilingual workflow.

We run B2B campaigns in English, Spanish, Arabic, occasionally German. The workflow we landed on:

STEP 1 — Source language draft

Always English. Written by a human (me, an AE, or a contractor).

STEP 2 — Cultural rewrite, not translation

Claude with explicit cultural instructions. Not 'translate this'. The instruction is: 'rewrite for a [Spanish/Arabic/German] B2B reader, preserving intent but adapting tone and structure.'

STEP 3 — Native review pass

A human native speaker reviews. Always. No exception.

STEP 4 — A/B against direct translation

Cultural rewrites outperform DeepL translation by 3-7x reply rate every quarter we've tested.

Most B2B teams either ship English everywhere, or translate via tool and call it done. The middle path costs more time. It also wins.

PM: the summer slowdown myth.

#Multilingual #B2BMarketing #Outbound

Q3 Playbook 4/20 — the summer slowdown is mostly a myth.

Every July-August, B2B teams blame the calendar.

'Buyers are on holiday. Pipeline always dies in summer.'

I've now run three Q3s. Pipeline doesn't die. Activity dies — and only some of it.

What actually happens:

→ Decision committees thin out for 2-3 week windows

→ Email reply rates drop 15-25%

→ But discovery calls hold steady — the people on calls are the ones not on holiday

→ Closed-won rates on deals that move are HIGHER than Q2

The opportunity:

→ Buyers who ARE working in August are doing real work, with less internal noise

→ A genuinely thoughtful email lands in a quieter inbox

→ Discovery calls that get booked tend to convert

What we do differently:

→ Don't drop volume. Increase specificity.

→ Move review-heavy steps to early September. Move outreach to August.

Don't pause the pipeline. Refine it.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #Outbound

Q3 Playbook 5/20 — refreshing your ICP for Q3.

Most teams set ICP in January and forget. By August it's stale.

Quick Q3 refresh, takes ~2 hours:

1. Pull last 6 months of closed-won deals.

2. List each deal's: industry sub-segment, company size, decision-maker title, deal size, sales cycle length.

3. Cluster: are 70% of wins coming from a tighter sub-segment than you'd expect? (Almost always yes.)

4. Re-write the ICP definition with that tighter sub-segment as primary.

5. Update your Clay scoring rubric to weight that sub-segment higher.

6. Run the new score on existing rows; promote the new top-scorers to action table.

What we found doing this in August 2025:

→ 70% of our wins were a sub-segment we hadn't formally targeted

→ Updating the rubric surfaced ~80 contacts we hadn't prioritised

→ Three of those 80 became deals by Q4

The data already lives in your CRM. The job is to listen to it.

#GTM #RevOps #B2B

Q3 Playbook 6/20 — the mistake that cost us €120k last Q3.

We had a strong campaign running into August 2025. Strong reply rate, strong booking rate, three deals at proposal stage.

I made a decision that felt smart at the time: pause the sequencing engine for 'summer'. Reduce noise, save credits.

What that meant:

→ New leads stopped getting nurtured for 3 weeks

→ Re-engagement on 4-month-cold deals didn't trigger

→ The pipeline I'd built lost momentum at the wrong time

Two of the three proposal-stage deals went cold. We didn't reach back fast enough when they returned in September. Both went to a competitor whose marketing didn't pause.

Real cost: ~€120k in lost revenue, by the most generous attribution.

Soft cost: an AE quit partly because of how the quarter felt.

Lesson:

Don't pause your engine in summer. Refine it. Reduce volume IF you must, but never stop nurture and re-engagement.

I've never made the mistake again. Sharing it so you don't either.

#GTM #LessonsLearned #B2BMarketing

Q3 Playbook 7/20 — the trade-show data intake flow.

Conference season heats up in autumn. Every event vendor sends a CSV with a different schema.

Our intake flow (n8n):

1. Watched folder — drop the CSV

2. Function node: detect schema (4 known formats, anything else routes to manual)

3. Normalise columns: name, email, company, title, country, event, booth, conversation note

4. Validate: drop rows with no email or no company

5. Push to Clay 'event' research table — tagged with event name and date

6. Trigger enrichment cycle on the new rows

7. Slack post to #events with stats: rows in, rows kept, rows quarantined

Total time per event: 4 minutes (vs 90+ minutes manual).

The trick that took longest to figure out:

Event-specific tagging. Without it, conference contacts get lost in the general flow. Tag by event + booth, then run conference-specific follow-up sequences in October.

Works for any B2B team running event marketing. The CSV is the universal constant.

PM: handling 'I'll get back to you in September'.

#n8n #ClayHQ #Events #RevOps

*'**see you in September**'*

Q3 Playbook 8/20 — what to do with 'I'll get back to you in September'.

Every August, every B2B team gets some version of this email.

Most teams handle it badly.

Bad way:

→ Move the deal to 'paused'

→ Set a calendar reminder for Sept 1

→ Send the same 'just circling back' email everyone else sends

Better way:

→ Reply same day with a SHORT, low-pressure note: 'Sounds good — when you're back, the question I'll want to ask is X. No rush.'

→ The X is a specific small question, not 'how does the rest of the year look'

→ Mid-August: send ONE useful thing — a relevant news item, a competitor mention — without asking anything

→ Early September: open with the X you flagged, not 'circling back'

Why this works:

You've planted a specific question they can prepare for, given them context to come back to, and avoided the generic September inbox flood.

Reopen rate on deals we handle this way: ~64%.

Reopen rate when we just send 'circling back': ~22%.

Plant your flag in August.

#Outbound #B2BSales #GTM

topic: Q3 Playbook 9/20 — HubSpot hygiene before EOY
body:
Q3 Playbook 9/20 — HubSpot field hygiene, before the EOY rush.

Most CRMs accumulate fields like a closet accumulates clothes. By August you're scared to touch anything.

Q3 hygiene checklist (do this once a year):

→ Audit contact properties. Anything not used in a workflow, sequence, view, or report in the last 90 days = candidate for archive.

→ Same for company properties.

→ Find duplicate fields (we had 'industry', 'industry_v2', 'sub_industry'). Consolidate.

→ Required fields review: are sales reps fighting them? They will skip them. Make required fields the truly necessary ones.

→ Workflow audit: list every active workflow. For each: 'does anyone trust the output?' If no, pause it.

→ Clean enum values: dropdowns drift. We had 5 spellings of 'United States'. Standardise.

Time investment: ~half a day.

Time saved later: every report you run for the next 6 months becomes more trustworthy.

Stale fields are silent revenue killers. They're why your forecasts feel off.

PM: the EOW digest that runs itself.

#HubSpot #RevOps #MarketingOps

Q3 Playbook 10/20 — the end-of-week digest that runs itself.

Friday 5pm. n8n cron fires.

What it does:

1. Queries HubSpot: deals created, deals closed-won, deals closed-lost (this week, last week, MoM trend)

2. Queries Clay: rows added to research, rows promoted to action

3. Queries Apollo: emails sent, replies, reply rate vs prior week

4. Queries our n8n monitoring flow: any flow errors this week, top 3

5. Claude reads all of the above and writes a 250-word digest in our voice. Honest, includes negative trends. No spin.

6. Posts to #leadership Slack channel + emails to the 4 leaders who don't live in Slack.

Cost: €0.04 per digest in Claude tokens.

Time saved: ~90 minutes/week of someone (me) writing the same email.

Bonus: leadership reads it. Because it's short and honest, not a marketing-team puff piece.

Friday digest is the highest-leverage piece of marketing infrastructure I've ever built. Not because it's complex. Because it gives leadership the truth on a schedule.

Week 1 of push wraps. 10 down, 10 to go.

#n8n #Claude #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: Q3 Playbook 11/20 — outbound copy that beats AI filters
body:
Q3 Playbook 11/20 — outbound copy that gets read in 2026.

Buyers are tuned to the LLM-template style. Generic openers, perfect grammar, three-bullet middle, soft CTA.

If your email looks like that, it gets archived in 1.4 seconds.

What works now:

→ Specifics that no template could have produced. A line about their actual job posting. A reference to their actual conference talk.

→ Imperfect grammar (genuinely). One short sentence. A fragment.

→ A claim that requires courage. 'I think your competitor is about to do X — happy to be wrong.'

→ Brevity that almost feels rude. 60 words. No 'hope this finds you well.'

What kills replies in 2026:

→ 'I came across your profile and was impressed by...'

→ Three bullet points listing benefits

→ Calendar links in the first email

→ Anything starting with 'Quick question'

If your email could be sent to 100 people unchanged, it shouldn't be sent at all.

PM: account-based vs contact-based.

#Outbound #B2BMarketing #ColdEmail

topic: Q3 Playbook 12/20 — when to switch ABM ↔ contact-based
body:
Q3 Playbook 12/20 — account-based vs contact-based outbound. When to use which.

ACCOUNT-BASED (target the company, multi-thread):

Use when…

→ Deal size > €100k

→ Buying committee is 5+ people

→ Sales cycle is 3+ months

→ Decision is structural, not personal

CONTACT-BASED (target the individual, fast loop):

Use when…

→ Deal size < €50k

→ Single decision-maker exists

→ Sales cycle under 60 days

→ Decision is personal preference

We run BOTH — and most teams running both run them poorly.

The split:

→ 80% of pipeline volume, 20% of revenue: contact-based for early-stage smaller deals

→ 20% of pipeline volume, 80% of revenue: account-based for the strategic deals

Most teams accidentally run an ABM motion on small deals (overkill) and a contact-based motion on huge deals (under-resourced).

Q3 fix: take a hard look at your last 10 closed-lost deals. Did they need a different motion than you ran? Probably yes.

#ABM #Outbound #B2BMarketing #GTM

Q3 Playbook 13/20 — the €40/month stack that runs our Q3 outbound.

Yes, €40. Itemised:

→ n8n: self-hosted on a €5/month VPS. Total cost: €5

→ Clay: shared workspace, ~€0/month at our usage in this stack slice

→ Apollo: existing seat, no incremental cost

→ Claude API: ~€20/month for the outbound-specific use cases

→ HubSpot: existing seat

→ A small custom dashboard hosted on a €5 VPS

→ Domain + minor things: ~€10

Total: €40/month for the engine that runs the outbound layer.

What's NOT in this number:

→ Apollo + HubSpot seat costs (would be there with or without this stack)

→ My salary (the actual scarce resource)

Why this matters:

Most teams overspend on tools because they're afraid to wire them themselves.

Wire it yourself once and tools become commodity. Leverage moves to your judgement.

PM: pricing conversations.

#GTM #RevOps #MarketingOps

Q3 Playbook 14/20 — structured pricing vs free-form.

B2B deals don't have a pricing page. Custom configurations, financing options. The question is whether to share structure early or keep it loose.

We've tested both. Structured wins.

STRUCTURED:

After the second discovery call, we share a one-page 'pricing structure' document. Not a quote. The structure: what the base costs, what the modules add, what services include.

Why this works:

→ Buyers can socialise it internally without us in the room

→ Procurement gets fewer surprises later, which means fewer last-minute deal blocks

→ It signals confidence — we know what we're worth

FREE-FORM (what we used to do):

Custom quote per buyer, only after extensive discovery, often surprising procurement.

→ Felt safer. Was actually slower.

→ Lost deals to competitors who were clearer about price upfront.

Counter-intuitive bit: sharing pricing structure earlier shortened our average sales cycle by ~3 weeks.

Confidence converts.

#B2BSales #Pricing #GTM

topic: Q3 Playbook 15/20 — reading buyer signals at events
body:
Q3 Playbook 15/20 — reading buyer signals at conferences.

Conference floors are noisy. Your booth gets 200 visitors over 3 days. 90% are window-shopping. 10% are buyers. Your job is to tell them apart in 90 seconds.

Signals that say 'buyer':

→ Asks about specific use cases within the first 60 seconds

→ References a pain ('our current setup struggles with X')

→ Shows up with one or two colleagues, not alone

→ Comes back to the booth a second time on day 2

Signals that say 'student / consultant / curious':

→ 'Tell me about what you do' (open-ended)

→ Wants the brochure but no business card exchange

→ Photos of the booth without the demo

What we do:

→ Two-question filter at the booth: 'what's the bottleneck?' + 'who else is involved in solving it?'

→ Buyers get a 20-min demo. Others get a brochure + email follow-up if they want it.

→ Day 3 morning: 30-min team huddle ranking all leads. Top 20% get a personal email by Saturday.

PM: Q3 dashboard that AEs use.

#Events #B2BSales #FieldMarketing

topic: Q3 Playbook 16/20 — Q3 dashboard AEs use
body:
Q3 Playbook 16/20 — building a Q3 dashboard that AEs actually use.

Most AE dashboards are designed for managers. AEs ignore them.

Ours is the opposite. Built for the AE's morning.

What's on it (one screen, no scrolling):

→ Top: deals at proposal stage with no activity in 5+ days. Sorted by deal size. Click → opens HubSpot to that deal.

→ Middle-left: deals waiting on me (action required from buyer). Tagged with last-touch date.

→ Middle-right: deals waiting on buyer (action required from us). Tagged with deadline.

→ Bottom-left: re-engagement signals from this week (the n8n flow output).

→ Bottom-right: my upcoming calls today + briefings (Claude-generated).

What's NOT on it:

→ Pipeline coverage ratios

→ Forecast charts

→ Anything that requires interpretation

Why it works: it tells the AE the next move. It doesn't make them think about the next move.

AEs open it 4-6 times a day. Manager dashboards live 2 weeks before being abandoned.

Build for the user, not the role above the user.

#RevOps #B2BSales #Dashboards

Q3 Playbook 17/20 — the sales-to-CS handoff at quarter-close.

Q3 → Q4 transition is where deals get handed from sales to CS. It's also where most companies leak retention.

Bad handoff:

→ Sales closes deal Friday Sept 26

→ CS picks it up Monday Oct 6 (10 days later)

→ CS doesn't know the buyer's politics, the technical edge cases, or the promise made at close

→ Customer's first 30 days feel like starting over

Good handoff:

→ Three-way kickoff call within 5 business days of close, every time

→ A standardised handoff doc the AE writes (Claude-assisted template, AE edits before sending)

→ The AE stays involved as escalation path for 60 days

→ 30/60/90 customer health check-ins, owned by CS, surfaced to the AE

What we wrote into our process this year: the AE's compensation has a 60-day clawback if onboarding stalls due to a scope mismatch from close. Painful for the AE, sharpens close-side scoping enormously.

The handoff is where customer experience starts. Plan it.

#B2BSales #CustomerSuccess #RevOps

topic: Q3 Playbook 18/20 — when to kill a campaign
body:
Q3 Playbook 18/20 — knowing when to kill a campaign mid-quarter.

Hardest decision in marketing.

Most teams keep losing campaigns alive too long because killing them feels like admitting failure.

Our internal rule (took two quarters of bad calls to land here):

Kill the campaign if, by week 4 of an 8-week run, AT LEAST TWO of these are true:

→ Reply rate is <60% of forecast

→ Booked-call rate is <50% of forecast

→ AEs are reporting low-quality conversations even on the booked calls

→ Cost-per-meaningful-conversation is >2x baseline

Why all four matter:

→ Reply rate alone can mislead (high replies, all 'wrong person')

→ Quality input from AEs catches what numbers miss

→ The cost lens forces honesty about whether we're learning enough

The post-mortem is non-negotiable. Within 5 days of killing, we write a 1-page 'what we learned' doc. Most of those become next quarter's better campaign.

Killing a campaign well is a skill. Build it deliberately.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #LessonsLearned

topic: Q3 Playbook 19/20 — October prep, in August
body:
Q3 Playbook 19/20 — October prep, in August.

October is the most expensive month in B2B if you arrive at it unprepared. Conferences are running, EOY budgets get talked about, every team you sell to is closing their own quarters.

Prep checklist, mid-August:

→ AUDIENCE: refresh ICP scoring. Done by end-August.

→ CONTENT: lock the October content calendar today. 8 weeks of theme + 3 carousels in draft.

→ CAMPAIGNS: pick the ONE big campaign for Q4. By August 20, the brief is written.

→ EVENTS: register for any October conference now. Booth materials ordered Aug 25.

→ TEAM: every AE has a Q4 plan in writing by Aug 31. Number, account list, two strategic moves.

→ TOOLS: any new platform you're considering? Choose by Aug 31, set up in September. Don't onboard during October.

→ DASHBOARDS: build the Q4 dashboard now while volume is lower.

Hardest one: holding the line on the August 31 deadline. Stuff slips. People are away. Hold it anyway.

Teams that arrive at October prepared spend October running. Teams that arrive unprepared spend October explaining.

PM tomorrow: the Q3 retrospective template. Final post.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #Quarterly

Q3 Playbook 20/20 — quarterly retrospective template. Last post.

Most retrospectives turn into either victory laps or blame sessions. Neither is useful.

Our template (one page, takes 90 minutes with the team):

1. NUMBERS (15 min, no commentary)

Pipeline created, deals closed-won, closed-lost, average cycle time, win rate, CAC. Vs forecast and last quarter. Read aloud, no analysis yet.

2. WHAT WE BET ON (15 min)

List the bets we explicitly made entering Q3. For each: did it pay off? How would we know?

3. WHAT BROKE (20 min)

Three things that broke that we didn't predict. NOT 'the market was hard' — operational specifics.

4. WHAT WORKED (15 min)

Three things that worked better than expected. Brutal about ranking in honesty order, not enthusiasm order.

5. THREE BETS FOR Q4 (20 min)

What we'll do differently. Each bet has a measurement criterion before we leave the room.

6. THE SINGLE BET (5 min)

If we could only do ONE thing differently in Q4, what? Vote. Discuss. Land.

Series wraps. 20 posts, 2 weeks. Recovery starts Monday.

#GTM #Retrospective #Frameworks

Push mode wrapped Friday. Some numbers, since I said I would:

→ 20 posts, 2 weeks, no missed days

→ Total impressions across the 20: ~340k

→ Followers gained: 1,420 (target was 1,200)

→ Top post: the €40 stack post (Aug 11 AM) — 47k impressions

→ Worst post: the customer-success handoff one — 6k impressions (too internal-process for the audience)

→ DM inbound: 14 unprompted, 5 from ICP-relevant buyers

What I'd do differently next push:

→ Less internal-process content. Audience cares about externally-visible work.

→ More carousels. Text-only push posts hit a ceiling around 12k.

→ Pre-write all 20 with one week buffer (I had 4 days). Recovery is real.

Now: 1 week of recovery. Base mode (3 posts). Then back to Growth through September.

Hardest part of push wasn't writing. It was not panicking when the third post in a row didn't pop.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

topic: Things you learn about yourself in push mode
body:
Things you learn about yourself when you publish 2 posts a day for two weeks:

1. You have more to say than you think — but only after the polished stuff runs out.

Day 4 the easy posts are gone. Day 8 you're writing things you didn't know you believed.

2. Your worst post is usually the one you spent the most time on.

Polish covers up confused thinking. Roughness reveals it.

3. Comments are louder than analytics.

One thoughtful comment beats 10k impressions for energy. Numbers and morale don't track the same way.

4. The audience can smell exhaustion.

Around day 9 my writing got tighter, not because I planned it, but because tired people write fewer adjectives. Engagement went up.

5. You become more honest under volume.

When you have to publish daily, the energy to perform runs out. What's left is the actual you.

Nobody told me this before push mode #1. Sharing in case you try one yourself.

Recovery week. Easy posts only.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

topic: Recovery — what one push taught me
body:
Saturday morning post. Recovery week thoughts.

Two weeks of 2 posts a day taught me something I didn't expect:

Volume is a stress test for sincerity.

When you post once a week, every post can be polished. Themed. Performed.

When you post twice a day for two weeks, the polish runs out. The themed angle runs out. What's left is what you actually think.

Halfway through the push, my posts shifted. Less 'how to' and more 'here's what I'm uncertain about'. Less playbook, more honest.

Engagement followed. The two highest-performing posts of the push were the two most uncertain ones — the €120k mistake post, and the cost-of-consistency post.

Lesson: an audience can smell when you're performing. Volume is what burns the performance off.

Three more push modes to come. I'm going into them with that lesson held tight.

Back to Growth mode (5/week) Monday.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

September theme: Industry POV.

After three months of 'how I do it' content, time to take some positions.

The plan:

→ 5 posts/week throughout September

→ At least one strong public position per week — modern B2B GTM, outbound, multilingual marketing, AI in revenue ops

→ Mid-month: a 'state of B2B GTM' carousel

What I'm trying to earn:

Citations. Reposts from senior creators. The kind of position-having that makes someone tag you in a debate.

Risk:

Strong positions get pushback. I'm OK with that — positions without disagreement aren't positions.

Constraint I'm setting myself: every POV post has to include either real numbers from my work, or an honest reason I might be wrong. No naked opinion.

Following along is free. Disagreeing in the comments is encouraged.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #BuildInPublic

Quick stack post — the infrastructure I use to post 5x a week without losing my mind.

→ A Notion table of 'post ideas' (currently 47 entries). Anything I see in conversation, in a metric, in a customer story — goes here. Most never become posts.

→ A 'drafts' page in Notion. ~10-15 posts in flight at any time, in various states of doneness.

→ Scheduled-post buffer of 5 days minimum. If buffer dips below 5, I prioritise writing over engagement that week.

→ Buffer or LinkedIn native scheduling.

→ A weekly Sunday review: read last week's metrics, kill 1 post idea, draft 2 new ones.

Total time on infrastructure: ~60 minutes per week.

Without it, posting daily is impossible. With it, it's just a habit.

Most people who quit posting after a month quit because of infrastructure friction, not lack of ideas. Build the system before you need it.

#ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips #BuildInPublic

topic: Why most B2B content sounds the same
body:
Why most B2B content sounds the same — and how to break out:

Most B2B teams write content the same way:

→ Topic chosen by SEO keyword research

→ Outline written by junior marketer

→ Draft written by content agency or AI

→ Edited to remove anything 'too opinionated'

→ Published with three CTAs

Result: a beige internet of indistinguishable B2B blog posts.

What stands out:

→ Topic chosen by what your team actually thinks about

→ Outline argued out in a meeting, not pulled from search

→ Draft written by the person who has the opinion

→ Edited to make the opinion SHARPER, not safer

→ Published with one specific question for readers

If a competitor could publish your content with their logo swapped in, your content has nothing to defend.

B2B content's enemy isn't AI. It's safe-by-committee.

Operator-led content is the answer.

#ContentMarketing #B2BMarketing #ThoughtLeadership

topic: Marketing operations is the hidden bottleneck
body:
Marketing operations is the hidden bottleneck in most B2B revenue orgs.

Sales gets the spotlight. Product gets the budget. Marketing gets the brief.

But marketing OPS — the people wiring the systems together — is where revenue actually scales or stalls.

Symptoms of broken marketing ops:

→ AEs don't trust the CRM

→ Reports take 2 hours to pull and still feel approximate

→ Tools that don't talk to each other

→ Lead routing that breaks every quarter

→ Nobody knows which campaigns drove which deals

When marketing ops works:

→ AEs check the dashboard before their morning coffee

→ Reports auto-generate Friday afternoon

→ The stack is small, integrated, and trusted

→ Lead routing runs untouched for months

→ Closed-won attribution is honest, not optimised

Companies that invest in marketing ops 6 months before they need to don't outgrow the problem. Companies that delay it spend years catching up.

If your CMO doesn't have a marketing-ops lead in their direct reports, you have a future problem.

#MarketingOps #RevOps #GTM

August recap. Three months of posting.

Numbers:

→ 28 posts shipped (push + recovery + normal)

→ Net new followers: ~2,100

→ Total following: ~12,400 (up from ~9,000 in May)

→ Best month for engagement rate (~6.4%)

→ First inbound DM from a Fortune 500 lead

What worked:

→ The push mode. Burned me out a bit but the audience grew measurably.

→ Carousels. 4x the saves of text posts.

→ Frameworks with diagrams (4-Layer Clay Map still getting saves 3 months later).

What didn't:

→ Internal-process posts (handoffs, dashboards) — too inside-baseball.

→ Late-afternoon posts. Reach drops noticeably after 11am CET.

September: 5/week, theme is Industry POV. Strong positions, stronger backup data. Less 'how I do it', more 'here's what I think'.

We're 25% of the way to the 25k follower target. Steady.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

Most B2B segmentation is broken. We've been doing it the same way for 15 years.

Standard segmentation:

→ Industry

→ Company size

→ Geography

→ Maybe a tech-stack tag

Same campaign goes to all of them. Same hook. Same offer.

What's missing — the segmentation that actually predicts buying behaviour:

→ TRIGGER (new VP, funding, regulation deadline, partner announcement)

→ STAGE (not aware → aware → searching → comparing → deciding)

→ SHAPE (single buyer vs committee, top-down vs bottom-up)

→ SEASON (most B2B has predictable budget cycles)

Score every prospect on those four dimensions. Run different campaigns at each combination. Reply rates climb.

The segmentation revolution everyone's been promising doesn't need new data. It needs smarter columns and the courage to act on them differently.

What's the most useful column you've added to your segmentation in the last 12 months?

#B2BMarketing #ABM #GTM #Segmentation

topic: What I do when I have no idea what to post
body:
What I do when I sit down to write and have nothing.

It happens at least once a week.

My process:

1. Open my Notion 'observations' file. (Things I noticed in the last 7 days that bothered me, surprised me, or confused me. Just bullet points.)

2. Pick the one that still bothers me 3 days later. That's the post.

3. Write the headline first. The headline is the post. If the headline isn't sharp, the body won't save it.

4. Write the worst possible draft. 5-10 minutes. Ugly, rambly, unedited.

5. Cut 40% of it. Almost always the right move.

6. Find the ONE specific number, name, or example I can add. Without it, the post is forgettable.

7. Read it aloud. If it sounds like marketing copy, rewrite.

Total time: ~45 minutes per post. Some take 90.

The trick isn't inspiration. It's a system that makes 'no ideas' impossible.

Save this if you're trying to start writing.

#ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips #BuildInPublic

*'**t replace GTM ops*

AI agents won't replace GTM ops.

They'll replace the GTM ops people who don't use them.

Two years ago the role was Salesforce admin + a few HubSpot workflows.

Today it's wiring Clay tables to LLM outputs into n8n flows that update a CRM and trigger Slack alerts.

If your day still looks like list-cleaning and report-pulling — you're at risk.

If you can wire an API, write a prompt, and ship an n8n flow that saves 8 hours/week — your trajectory just bent up sharply.

Skills that compound:

→ Reading API docs

→ Prompt engineering as a real discipline

→ Workflow design — what to automate, what to leave manual

→ Light coding (functions, conditionals, transforms)

None requires a CS degree. Requires curiosity and 8-12 weekends.

The roles paying double in 2027 are looking now.

#RevOps #MarketingOps #CareerJourney

The B2B content equation that explains why most posts flop:

REACH = SPECIFICITY × COURAGE

Specificity = real numbers, named tools, dated experiences, traceable claims.

Courage = positions that cost you something, opinions you'd defend in a 1:1, things your competitors can't copy.

If specificity is low: nobody saves the post (it's vague).

If courage is low: nobody comments (it's safe).

If both are low: silence.

Both high: the post that compounds for months.

How to test your draft:

→ Specificity test: count the numbers, named tools, dates. Less than 3? Add more.

→ Courage test: would you say this in a 1:1 with someone you respect? If you'd hedge, hedge less in the post too.

What kills B2B content:

→ Generic advice (low specificity)

→ 'Industry trend' observations (low courage)

→ Anything that sounds like a press release (zero × zero)

Save this. Run your next draft through it.

#ContentMarketing #B2BMarketing #Frameworks

topic: What I post about, what I skip
body:
Three months in. People keep asking what I won't post about.

WILL POST:

→ Stack details, numbers, screenshots

→ Mistakes, killed campaigns, failed flows

→ Frameworks I've built and tested

→ B2B GTM positions I'd defend in a 1:1

→ BTS work cleared by my team

→ Honest reflections on what's hard

WON'T POST:

→ Customer details without permission

→ Internal politics or hiring/firing

→ Vague motivational content

→ 'Hot takes' I wouldn't say to my CEO

→ Other people's bad work

→ Predictions I have no skin in

Your list doesn't have to match. But having one helps when you're tempted to post in the wrong moment.

Today's the start of Industry POV month — week 2.

#BuildInPublic #LinkedInTips

topic: Clay column we deleted that nobody missed
body:
Last week we deleted a Clay column that had been there for 7 months.

Nobody noticed for 4 days.

The column: 'last website visit date'.

Sounded useful. Pulled from a tracking pixel, refreshed daily, ~640 rows.

Cost: ~€18/month + ~5 minutes per refresh.

Produced: zero workflow triggers, zero AE conversations, zero reports.

It existed because someone (me, six months ago) said 'we should track this'. We never built the workflow.

Rule: every Clay column should justify its presence with a workflow within 30 days. If not, archive.

I do this audit quarterly. Every quarter I delete 3-5 columns nobody misses.

Most B2B GTM ops have the same accumulation problem. Tools, fields, automations, dashboards. Half of them are decoration.

Audit before you add. What's hiding in your tables?

#ClayHQ #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: The conversation that changed my career
body:
The conversation that changed my career, in 4 sentences:

I was complaining to a senior colleague about a manual marketing task that ate 6 hours a week.

She said: 'have you Googled the API for that tool?'

I had not. I assumed APIs were for engineers.

Three weekends later I'd automated the whole thing in n8n and saved myself most of a working day every week.

What I learned that month:

→ The line between 'marketer' and 'developer' is thinner than your title suggests

→ Most marketing tools have public APIs and almost nobody on the marketing side uses them

→ The willingness to look at a docs page is what separates compounding marketers from stuck ones

→ One conversation with the right person can reframe an entire career

I've since had that exact conversation with at least 5 junior marketers. Most of them now ship n8n flows.

If you're a marketer reading this and you've never opened an API doc — open one this week. The career you have in 2027 starts with that page.

#CareerJourney #MarketingOps #Mentorship

*'**t dead*

Trade shows aren't dead. Trade-show marketing is dead.

I keep hearing 'we're cutting the conference budget' because last year produced no measurable pipeline.

Pipeline didn't fail to show up. Marketing failed.

What kills conference ROI:

→ Booth designed for browsing, not buying

→ Lead capture as a CSV in someone's inbox

→ Generic follow-up to everyone, treated identically

→ No content angle that lasts beyond the event

What works:

→ Targeted invites 4 weeks before

→ Two-question filter at the booth

→ Same-day intake into your CRM

→ Personalised follow-up within 5 days, NOT a templated 'great meeting you'

→ Conference becomes a content cluster — before, during, AFTER

Real number from us: of €2.4M Q4 pipeline last year, 38% touched a conference somewhere in the journey.

If your conference is broken, fix the marketing around it. Don't kill the channel.

#Events #B2BMarketing #FieldMarketing

Why marketing teams ship slow — and what to do about it.

The five reasons I see most often:

1. TOO MANY APPROVERS

Every campaign needs sign-off from CMO, legal, brand, product, sales. Each one adds days. Fix: name a single 'campaign owner' empowered to ship; others give input, not approval.

2. TOO MUCH POLISH

First draft → 3 review cycles → designer → review → final. Most of those cycles add nothing. Fix: ship version 0.7 in public; iterate live.

3. TOO MUCH STRATEGY UPFRONT

30-page strategy decks before the first campaign launches. Fix: ship one campaign in week 1, learn, then write strategy informed by reality.

4. TOOL FRICTION

Stack so complex that launching anything is a 5-step sequence across 4 tools. Fix: one bigger audit, fewer tools.

5. NO RECURRING CADENCE

Every campaign is a special event. Fix: a weekly publishing rhythm makes the next thing easier than the last.

Slow marketing teams aren't lazy. They're stuck in process scar tissue.

Cut the scar tissue. Don't add another tool.

#MarketingOps #B2BMarketing #GTM

#GTM #B2BMarketing #BuildInPublic

An n8n flow I usually don't talk about — but it might be the most useful one I run.

Every Friday 5pm, n8n compiles a 'state of the business' message and sends it to me alone.

What it includes:
