Basically a personal CTO digest.

Why this matters:

Most marketing operators run their own infrastructure blind. They build automations, forget about them, and find out in October that something has been silently failing since August.

11 nodes. Saves quarterly disasters.

If you run automation and don't have a 'state of my stack' digest — build one before you need it. The week you'll wish you had it is the week you'll find out it broke 3 weeks ago.

#n8n #MarketingOps #RevOps

topic: State of Modern B2B GTM — Sept 2026
body:
The big one. State of Modern B2B GTM — September 2026. (Carousel, 14 slides — first slide in the comments.)

The argument:

B2B GTM in 2026 looks fundamentally different from 2023, and most teams haven't caught up.

Five things that worked in 2023 but don't anymore:

1. Volume outbound (templates die in spam)

2. MQL-driven funnels (anonymous research broke them)

3. Generic LinkedIn ads (CTR collapsed)

4. Webinar-then-nurture (attention spans changed)

5. Single-language campaigns (multilingual buyers expect more)

Five things that work in 2026:

→ Operator-led content (real people on real platforms)

→ Trigger-based outbound (small lists, big specificity)

→ AI-researched, human-written outreach

→ Cultural rewrite (not translation) for international markets

→ Owned-channel community (smaller, more loyal)

Three mistakes I see:

→ Buying tools instead of skills

→ Treating AI as a generation engine, not a research engine

→ Underinvesting in marketing ops

Plus: a 5-dimension maturity rubric to score your team.

Save. Repost if useful.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #ModernGTM

topic: Hot take — n8n vs Zapier at scale
body:
Hot take that'll annoy Zapier fans:

If you spend more than €200/month on Zapier, you're probably overspending by 10x.

Zapier is brilliant for:

→ Tiny teams without engineering capacity

→ Single-step automations

→ Quick prototypes

Zapier breaks at:

→ Per-task pricing as you scale (we'd be at €1,200+/month)

→ Multi-step logic with conditionals

→ Custom HTTP integrations

→ Version control

n8n self-hosted runs on a €5/month VPS. Same flows, more flexibility, Git-versioned.

Setup: about a weekend if you're new.

Counter: 'we don't have engineering to maintain it'.

Fair. But running n8n self-hosted is closer to running a Notion than a database.

I'm not saying everyone should switch. I'm saying most people running Zapier at scale haven't honestly compared.

#n8n #MarketingAutomation #RevOps

Wednesday's carousel went up. 48 hours later:

→ ~62k impressions

→ 1,400+ saves

→ ~280 net new followers from this single post

→ DMs from operators in 7 different sectors

→ A repost from a senior creator I respect

What surprised me:

Most DMs aren't from one sector. They're from operators in different industries with the same GTM problem. The thesis generalises further than I thought.

What I'm now considering:

→ A series of similar 'state of GTM' carousels for adjacent topics (outbound, ABM, marketing ops)

→ An open Notion doc the community can contribute to

→ A small Slack for modern B2B GTM operators, under 50 people

Don't know yet which are good ideas. Marking the moment.

If you're thinking 'I should do a state-of-something carousel': do it. The audience is bigger and quieter than you think.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

topic: The hardest part of working in 3 languages
body:
The hardest part of working in three languages every day isn't the languages.

It's switching between three completely different professional cultures within the same hour.

An English-speaking call: agenda, time-boxed, decision at the end.

A Spanish-speaking call: relationship first, business in the back half, decision often after the call.

An Arabic-speaking call: context-heavy opening, indirect disagreement, the actual answer often arrives later by message.

Same person can be in all three calls in one day. The exhausting part is being culturally fluent in all of them, not linguistically fluent.

What I've learned:

→ Language fluency is the entry ticket. Cultural fluency is the actual work.

→ 'I'm efficient' in one culture is 'I'm rude' in another.

→ The biggest career compounding I've had is from being able to read the room across cultures, not from any tool I learned.

If you're hiring multilingual talent — value cultural translation as much as language. They're different skills. The second is rarer.

If you're considering working across cultures yourself — it's worth it. Just don't underestimate how tiring 'context-switching' is.

#Multilingual #CareerJourney #Leadership

We score every campaign on a 5-dimension rubric internally. Sharing it.

1. SPECIFICITY (1-10)

Generic = low. 'B2B SaaS founders fundraising in Q4' = high. Vague segments are decoration.

2. OPERATOR VOICE (1-10)

Written like someone doing the work, or in marketing voice? Operator voice always wins on technical content.

3. PROOF DENSITY (1-10)

Per 500 words, how many real numbers, named customers, dated examples? Buyers count proof, not adjectives.

4. CHANNEL FIT (1-10)

Channels your buyers actually inhabit (LinkedIn, podcasts, conferences) — not display ads.

5. ONLY-YOU ANGLE (1-10)

If a competitor could publish the same content, you score zero here.

Total: 50 points. Below 35, we redo.

Use it on yours. Lowest dimension tells you where the next 90 days of work goes.

Save the post. Run it on your last campaign honestly.

#B2BMarketing #Frameworks #ContentMarketing

*'**t put in posts*

Honest BTS — the things I deliberately leave out of my posts.

→ Customer names. Even when I have permission, I usually anonymise. Future-me wants flexibility.

→ Exact deal sizes. I'll say 'mid-six figures'. I won't say 'this specific deal was €387,420'.

→ Internal team conflict. Real, frequent, never my story to tell.

→ My boss's decisions, even when I disagree. Loyalty is private; disagreement is private.

→ Specific competitor weaknesses. I'll critique a CATEGORY, not a named company.

→ Numbers I can't reproduce. If I can't show the dashboard, I don't claim the number.

→ My salary. (I get asked surprisingly often.)

→ Anything that would surprise my CEO if she read it Sunday morning.

Why post about what I don't post about?

Because the scariest part of building publicly isn't writing — it's deciding what's off-limits. Most people overshare or undershare. Both fail.

Build your own list before you need it. Know your line.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

*'**thought leadership**'** fails*

Most B2B 'thought leadership' fails the same way.

The author is senior. The post sounds like:

→ 'In today's rapidly evolving landscape...'

→ Trend observation + inspirational tone

→ Three things readers 'should consider'

→ Soft CTA suggesting a conversation

Nobody learns. Nobody cites. Nobody saves.

What works instead:

1. SPECIFICITY

Not 'B2B sales is changing'. 'Cycles in mid-market SaaS moved from 9 months in 2022 to 6.5 months in 2025.'

2. POSITIONS YOU'D DEFEND

'We stopped running ABM for deals under €100k and grew pipeline 40%.'

3. NUMBERS YOU OWN

Not market reports. Numbers from your work.

4. BEING WRONG SOMETIMES

If you're never publicly wrong, you're never publicly right either.

5. CRAFT

Read it aloud. If it sounds like a press release, rewrite.

Thought leadership isn't a tone. It's a discipline of saying things that cost you something.

#ContentMarketing #B2BMarketing #ThoughtLeadership

Closing September with the thesis I've been circling all month:

B2B doesn't need more journalists or marketers. It needs more operator-creators.

An operator-creator:

→ Does the actual work

→ Writes about it publicly, often

→ Connects practical detail to broader pattern

→ Names things they don't have permission to name

In SaaS, they're everywhere. Read any week of LinkedIn posts about Clay, n8n, HubSpot — operators teaching operators.

In most other B2B sectors (finance ops, supply chain, fintech, healthcare tech, manufacturing tech), they're still rare.

Why it matters:

→ Talent flows toward visible categories

→ Capital follows visible categories

→ Customers learn from visible categories

The fix isn't a marketing campaign. It's individual operators choosing to write.

If you're a B2B operator reading this — write something. The category you work in is waiting.

topic: Why frameworks beat POV posts long-term
body:
Most B2B content is one-off. A post is a post.

Frameworks are different. A framework is reusable mental architecture.

Why operators should think in frameworks:

→ Compounds in the audience's memory

→ Becomes cite-able by others

→ Forces clarity in your own thinking

→ Creates language teams can adopt internally

Three rules I follow when naming a framework:

1. The name fits in one tweet

2. It comes from production, not theory

3. The diagram is one image, not a slide deck

Examples I keep close:

→ The 4-Layer Clay Map (mine, May)

→ April Dunford's positioning canvas

→ The Bowtie Funnel (Winning by Design)

→ Jobs-to-Be-Done (Christensen)

Frameworks aren't decoration. They're how operators teach each other at scale.

topic: Q4 starts — what most teams get wrong
body:
Q4 in B2B is where most teams get the calendar wrong.

Two common mistakes:

1. Treating Q4 as a sales push month

It's actually a positioning month. Buyers are scoping NEXT year's budgets. Q4 deals often close in Q1. Push too hard for Q4 closes and you'll get stalled deals.

2. Pausing marketing 'for the holidays'

Mid-December gets quiet. Late November and early December are some of the best engagement periods of the year. Inboxes are calmer, attention is higher, decision-makers are reflecting.

What we do differently:

→ Q4 marketing is biased toward 'plant the flag for Q1' — content for next year's budget conversations

→ Heavy outbound through mid-December, paused only Dec 22-Jan 5

→ January 5 is treated as a major launch moment, not a 'restart from cold'

Run your Q4 calendar like the start of next year, not the end of this one.

#GTM #B2BMarketing #Quarterly

Tomorrow I post the first named framework of October: The Saleh Stack.

What it is:

A 5-component mental model for any modern B2B GTM stack. Names what each component is FOR, not which tool sits there.

Why this matters:

Most stack posts are 'we use X, Y, Z'. Useful for one company. Useless if your tools differ.

A stack framework names the FUNCTIONS, so you can map any toolset.

I've been refining it for 18 months. Time to make it a thing.

topic: The Saleh Stack — 5 components of any modern B2B GTM stack
body:
The Saleh Stack. Five components every modern B2B GTM stack needs. Diagram in the comments.

1. SOURCE

Where new candidates enter your world. Apollo, ZoomInfo, scraping, referrals, conferences. NOT a destination — only an inflow.

2. RESEARCH

Where candidates get evaluated, enriched, scored. Patient and rich. Updates weekly. Clay sits here for most of us. Output: a confidence score and a 'next move'.

3. ACTION

Where the worthy candidates get worked. Lean. Fast. Strict. Apollo sequences, manual reach-outs, 1:1 emails. Output: replies, calls, meetings.

4. RECORD

The system of record. Reliable, not clever. HubSpot or Salesforce. Holds deals, owners, dates, dollars.

5. INTELLIGENCE

Where the loop closes. Dashboards, weekly digests, ICP rubric updates. Reads from RECORD, writes back to RESEARCH. Most teams skip this layer.

Most stacks are missing #5. That's why they don't compound.

Save the diagram. Map your stack. Find the gaps.

#GTM #Frameworks #ClayHQ #RevOps

topic: What customers teach you that strategy decks never will
body:
Most useful marketing input doesn't come from strategy decks.

It comes from 30 minutes on a call with a customer who's struggling.

Last week I sat in on a discovery call. The buyer used a phrase I'd never have invented in a brief:

'We don't have a tooling problem. We have a coordination problem.'

Eight words. Re-framed our entire next-quarter messaging.

Most B2B teams treat customer feedback as a CS problem. We treat it as a marketing input.

Where it enters our stack:

→ Every won deal: a 30-min call between AE and CS, recorded, transcribed

→ Every lost deal: a short async survey + AE notes

→ Every QBR: a structured 'what's working / what's not' review

Where it goes:

→ Notion 'voice of customer' database, tagged by industry, deal size, theme

→ Quarterly: read-through with the marketing team, find 3 patterns

→ Patterns become content angles, ICP refinements, sometimes product feedback

Marketing without a feedback loop is just guessing in public. The strategy deck doesn't fix it. The customer call does.

#B2BMarketing #CustomerVoice #MarketingOps

Updated version of the outbound diagnostic I posted in July.

Reply rate under 2%? You have one of three problems.

Run this 60-second triage:

Q1: What's your bounce rate?

→ >4% = DATA problem. Stop. Fix data before anything else.

→ <4% = continue.

Q2: What's your open rate?

→ <35% = DATA or DELIVERABILITY. Check from-domain warmup, subject lines.

→ 35-50% = continue.

Q3: When replies happen, are they 'wrong person'?

→ More than 30% wrong-person = TARGETING. Persona is misaligned.

→ Less = continue.

Q4: When AEs read 5 sample emails aloud, do they cringe?

→ Yes = COPY problem. Rewrite with one specific observation per email.

→ No = it's something deeper (offer, timing, market).

Most teams jump to copy because copy is fun to fix. Diagnose first.

Save this. Run it next time outbound stalls.

#Outbound #Frameworks #RevOps

topic: How to write for technical buyers
body:
How to write content for technical B2B buyers (engineers, RevOps, security, IT). A short framework.

Technical buyers DON'T want:

→ Inspirational tone

→ Vague trend observation

→ 'Future of X' framing

→ More than 3 adjectives in any sentence

Technical buyers DO want:

→ Specifics with units (€, %, ms, GB, hours)

→ Pre-conditions and edge cases stated

→ Tradeoffs acknowledged

→ Failure modes listed

→ A diagram, ideally

5-rule writing test:

1. Could this sentence have a number in it?

2. Did I name a tradeoff?

3. Did I tell them when this approach DOESN'T work?

4. Is there a unit attached to every claim?

5. Would another technical person steelman this?

If you can't pass 4 of 5, rewrite.

Technical buyers don't read most marketing because most marketing fails this test. The marketing they DO read passes it.

#B2BMarketing #ContentMarketing #TechnicalMarketing

topic: What 30 operators told me about the Saleh Stack
body:
After last week's Saleh Stack post, ~30 operators DM'd me with what their stack looks like. Patterns:

→ 90% are missing layer 5 (INTELLIGENCE / closing the loop). Most know it's missing.

→ 60% confuse SOURCE and ACTION (using Apollo as both, no separation).

→ 40% use HubSpot as RESEARCH (over-burdening the CRM with enrichment data).

→ Only 2 had a working backfeed loop (closed-won data improving ICP scoring).

Most common question: 'how do I add layer 5 without rebuilding everything?'

Pragmatic answer:

→ Start small. A weekly digest auto-generated from your CRM is layer 5 in its simplest form.

→ Add a manual quarterly: walk through last quarter's wins/losses, update one ICP attribute.

→ Once that's habitual, automate the quarterly into the digest.

Frameworks expose gaps. The Saleh Stack post wasn't 'here's our stack' — it was a mirror. Most teams don't have layer 5.

If yours doesn't, you're not alone. The gap is real and worth closing.

#GTM #RevOps #Frameworks

Framework drop. The 4-Layer Modern B2B Marketing Map. (Carousel, 10 slides.)

How to evaluate any B2B marketing function in 2026.

LAYER 1 — VOICE

Who's writing? Marketing agency? Internal marketer? Operator? Engineer? Operator + practitioner voices outperform pure marketing voice ~3x on technical content.

LAYER 2 — SUBSTANCE

How dense is the proof? Numbers, units, named examples, customer references. B2B buyers count proof, not adjectives.

LAYER 3 — DEPTH

How many layers down can a buyer go? Carousel → article → case study → demo. Buyers like rabbit holes when the surface intrigued them.

LAYER 4 — REACH

Where does this content live? LinkedIn, podcasts, conferences, YouTube. Reach is increasingly multi-channel.

Most B2B marketing fails at layer 1 (wrong voice) or layer 2 (insufficient proof). Layers 3 and 4 follow naturally if 1 and 2 are right.

Score yourself 1-10 on each. Lowest layer = next 90 days of work.

Save the carousel. Use it for your 2027 planning.

#Frameworks #B2BMarketing #ContentMarketing

*'**AI in marketing**'** will mean in 2027*

What 'AI in marketing' will mean in 2027 — versus 2024.

2024 version:

→ ChatGPT to draft content

→ AI tools to scale outbound generation

→ Automation of existing workflows

→ Mostly about productivity

2027 version (where I see it heading):

→ Research, not generation. AI reads more than it writes.

→ Smaller, sharper output. Volume game ended.

→ Customer-facing AI experiences (auditable, explainable, controlled).

→ AI-augmented operators, not AI-replaced operators.

→ Stack consolidation. Fewer tools, deeper integrations.

→ Local + privacy-first models for sensitive data.

What this means for marketers planning 2027:

→ Don't budget for 'AI tools' as a line item. Budget for capabilities the AI unlocks.

→ Hire people who can wire AI into existing workflows, not people who can prompt-engineer.

→ Reduce dependence on AI-generated content. Increase dependence on AI-researched insight.

The next year is going to look different from the last. Plan accordingly.

#AI #MarketingAutomation #GTM

After 14 months running Claude in production for marketing automation, the rules I'd give my younger self:

1. THE SYSTEM PROMPT IS 80% OF THE WORK

Spend an hour writing it. Re-read it weekly. Pin examples. Dictate output structure.

2. NEVER TRUST FREE-FORM JSON

Define the schema. Validate. Have a fallback for malformed responses.

3. SEPARATE READ FROM WRITE

AI summarising customer notes = great. AI writing customer-facing content unedited = still risky in 2026. Write for human review.

4. LOG EVERYTHING

Every input, every output, every cost. Without logs you can't debug a regression.

5. RATE-LIMIT YOURSELF

Add delays between calls. The bug you didn't predict will burn $400 of credits before you notice.

6. PIN THE MODEL VERSION

Don't use 'latest'. Pin the version. Re-evaluate quarterly.

7. WRITE A TEST PROMPT FILE

10-20 test inputs. Run after every prompt change. The discipline saves regressions.

Save this if you're building anything LLM-powered for B2B GTM.

#AI #Claude #MarketingAutomation

topic: What changed when I stopped trying to be impressive
body:
An honest reflection. What changed when I stopped trying to be impressive in posts.

Early posts: I tried to sound senior. Strategic. Like someone you'd hire as VP marketing.

Reach: medium.

Conversations: shallow.

Now: I try to sound like the operator I am. I write about what I broke this week. The number that surprised me. The mistake I'm correcting. The framework I built and refined three times.

Reach: 4x higher.

Conversations: real.

What changed in my head:

→ I realised audiences don't reward 'impressive'. They reward 'specific'.

→ The operator voice I was hiding is the voice the audience actually trusted.

→ Trying to sound senior dilutes both seniority and authenticity.

If you're early in your B2B marketing career and tempted to write like the SVP of demand gen — don't.

Write like you, with three months less experience than you had last year. Showing the climb is more useful than performing the summit.

Save this if you're hesitating to post.

#CareerJourney #ContentMarketing #BuildInPublic

Quick framework for any new piece of marketing work.

Before you ship a campaign, page, or post, the 3-Question Briefing Test:

Q1: WHO is this for?

Not 'CMOs at mid-market SaaS'. ONE person you can name. ONE buyer or one persona archetype with detail. If you can't name them, the work is too vague.

Q2: WHAT do you want them to think after they consume this?

One sentence. Not 'be impressed by our brand'. Something specific: 'their ICP-scoring approach is one I should evaluate against ours'.

Q3: WHAT'S the thing only this content can prove?

If a competitor could publish the same content, your content isn't doing the job. Find the angle ONLY you can credibly take.

If you can't pass all three in 90 seconds, the brief isn't ready.

I run this on every post before I publish. Half the time it sends me back to rewrite.

Save it. Pin it above your desk. The discipline pays.

#ContentMarketing #B2BMarketing #Frameworks

topic: Why most B2B marketing budgets are wrong
body:
Most B2B companies allocate marketing budget wrong.

Typical split I see:

→ 30% paid digital

→ 25% events

→ 20% content / website

→ 15% brand / PR

→ 10% tools

What I'd argue it should be:

→ 30% content (especially long-form, technical, operator-led)

→ 25% events (still highest-converting B2B channel)

→ 20% community + creator support (build a network of voices)

→ 15% paid digital (LinkedIn ads on tight ICP only)

→ 10% tools

The big shift: from paid digital to content + community.

Why:

→ Paid digital ROI in B2B has been declining for years

→ Content compounds. Year 2 of consistent content = real pipeline contribution

→ Community is the dark matter of B2B — peer recommendations beat any ad

Reallocate slowly. Start with 5-10% shift this quarter. Measure honestly. Adjust.

#B2BMarketing #GTM #DemandGen

A mentor I never met has shaped my career more than any I have.

April Dunford. I've never spoken to her. I've read every word she's written, watched every talk, listened to every podcast.

What she taught me through her work:

→ Positioning isn't messaging. It's the deliberate choice of context that makes your strengths matter.

→ Your competitors aren't always other companies. Sometimes they're 'do nothing' or 'build it yourself'.

I haven't met her. But every campaign I write, every messaging doc I edit, every demo question I help an AE prep for — has her thinking embedded.

topic: The Q4-to-Q1 bridge — 4 things to plan now
body:
Q4 is the wrong frame. Q4-to-Q1 is the right frame.

Every year, teams spend Q4 chasing closes and arrive at Q1 unprepared. Then Q1 becomes panic + planning instead of execution.

Plan these 4 things in November:

1. ICP REFRESH

Pull last 12 months of closed-won. Cluster by industry sub-segment, deal size, sales cycle. Update the ICP definition for next year.

2. CONTENT THEMES

Pick 4 quarterly themes for next year, not 12 monthly themes. Quarterly themes give content room to breathe.

3. CHANNEL BETS

Last year's channel mix isn't next year's. Look at what compounded vs what stalled. Reallocate 10-20% of budget.

4. ONE BIG CAMPAIGN

Pick the ONE campaign for Q1. Brief written by mid-December. Nothing else gets a campaign-level brief until Q1 ends.

If you do this in November, January feels different. Calm. Focused.

If you skip it, January feels like every other January. Hectic. Reactive.

I'd rather have one calm January than five panicked ones.

topic: Half-Year Recap 4/20 — the post that flopped
body:
Half-Year Recap 4/20 — the post that flopped.

August 13 evening: 'Customer-success handoff at quarter-close'.

Genuinely useful content. ~6,200 impressions (vs my ~22k average that week). One of my worst-performing posts.

Why it flopped:

1. Too internal-process

Handoffs between sales and CS are real, but the audience reading my account is mostly marketers and operators, not sales managers. Wrong audience.

2. No specific number

I made it abstract instead of concrete. No 'we changed X and saw Y'. The post was advice, not proof.

3. PM post in push mode

PM posts in push mode are supposed to be lighter / more personal. This was a heavy operational post in a personal slot. Tone-mismatch.

4. No diagram

If a process is worth posting, it's worth diagramming. Pure text on a process post tanks reach.

What I'd do differently:

→ Reframe as 'a marketer's view of the sales-to-CS handoff' (different angle for the audience I have)

→ Add a concrete number from a real handoff

→ Add a diagram

→ Save it for an AM tactical slot

Flops are data. The post taught me as much as the wins.

topic: Half-Year Recap 7/20 — the behaviour change that mattered most
body:
Half-Year Recap 7/20 — the behaviour change that mattered most.

It wasn't the posting itself.

It was the Sunday review.

Every Sunday for 26 weeks I've spent 60-90 minutes:

→ Reading last week's metrics

→ Killing one post idea that's gone stale

→ Drafting 2 new posts for the week ahead

→ Writing a 3-bullet 'lessons from last week' note in Notion

This habit, more than any individual post, is what made consistency possible.

Before this habit:

→ Mondays felt panicky (no plan)

→ Posts were last-minute

→ Quality was inconsistent

→ I wasn't learning from each week

After this habit:

→ Mondays feel calm (5 posts already in some draft state)

→ Posts get edited multiple times before publishing

→ Quality is consistent

→ Each week genuinely teaches me something

If you're considering posting consistently — the calendar habit is more important than any individual post technique.

Sundays are sacred now. They're when next week happens.

#BuildInPublic #Productivity #ContentMarketing

Half-Year Recap 8/20 — engagement strategies that worked.

1. Tier-1 daily engagement

Comment substantively on 5-7 senior creators every day, in the first hour after their post. Algorithm rewards proximity. Audience overlap follows.

2. Reply to every comment within 60 minutes of posting

Lifts the post in the feed. Builds relationship with the commenter. Most teams skip this.

3. DM 3-5 people after every post

Not 'pls engage'. A real message: 'Wrote this — curious what you think because of [specific reason].'

4. Public reposts of others' work

Once a week, share someone else's post with my own added angle. Builds reciprocity.

5. Show up in someone else's comments before posting yourself

Be visible in the broader conversation, not just on your own real estate.

What I tracked:

→ Days where I engaged > 60 minutes vs days where I just posted

→ Engagement-heavy days drove ~40% more reach the same week

→ Posts on days I'd engaged for 60+ min that week outperformed by ~25%

Posting alone is not enough. Engagement is the second half of growth.

#BuildInPublic #LinkedInTips

*'**t*

Half-Year Recap 9/20 — engagement strategies that didn't work.

1. Engagement pods

Joined two during May. Left both by July. The engagement was hollow — same 8 people commenting on each other's posts. Algorithm sees through it eventually. Quality of comments was low.

2. Generic comments at scale

Tried 30+ short comments per day in June. Reach didn't improve. Author replies didn't happen. Quality matters more than volume.

3. Tagging large creators in posts

Did this 3-4 times early. None of them engaged. Signals desperation more than respect.

4. Posting in 5+ niche groups

Joined ~12 LinkedIn groups in May. Cross-posted to several. Reach was minimal. Most B2B groups are quiet now.

5. 'Agree?' bait at the end of posts

Tried it. Felt cheap. Engagement marginally up but the wrong type — quick agreements, no depth. Stopped.

What I learned:

Engagement gimmicks have a half-life of weeks. Real conversation has a half-life of years.

If you're starting now: skip the gimmicks. Spend the time on substantive comments and DMs instead.

#BuildInPublic #LinkedInTips

Half-Year Recap 10/20 — the first inbound deal.

August 14. A DM from a sustainability lead at a mid-size European company.

Direct quote (paraphrased with permission):

'Been reading your stuff for two months. Curious whether your team could fit our use case. Open to a 30-min call.'

What I noticed:

→ They had read multiple posts before reaching out (mentioned three specifically)

→ They'd done internal research first

→ The DM was specific, not generic

→ They asked for a call, not a pitch

What happened:

→ Discovery call within a week

→ Technical demo with engineering 3 weeks later

→ Proposal in early October

→ Currently at procurement stage

Caveat: not closed yet. May or may not close. But the pipeline is real.

What I'd do differently if I were starting:

Don't expect inbound for 3-6 months. The first inbound for me happened at month 4. The audience needs to trust you before they DM you about a serious budget.

Patience compounds.

#BuildInPublic #B2BSales #InboundMarketing

Half-Year Recap 11/20 — what I underestimated about content.

1. How much business inbound posting attracts even when you're not asking

Recruiting offers. Speaking invites. Consulting requests. People want to work with operators who post.

2. How much consistency itself becomes proof

Showing up every week is a credential. Not a brilliant post. The posting itself.

3. How much community vs broadcast matters

I thought I was building an audience. I was actually building a network. Different thing. Network is who replies. Audience is who scrolls.

4. How transferable the lessons are across sectors

I assumed my niche was its own thing. Turns out the patterns generalise across B2B. The DMs from different industries proved this.

5. How much my own work improves because I write about it

Forcing myself to articulate what I built clarified what I built. Some of my most useful internal decisions came from drafting a post and realising the rationale didn't hold.

6. How fast trust forms when you're public + specific + honest

People I've never met DM me with real questions. Forms in months what private networking forms in years.

Underestimating these is normal. They're the kind of thing you can't believe until you've done six months.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

Half-Year Recap 12/20 — what I overestimated.

1. The cost of negative reactions to hot takes

I worried about backlash on opinionated posts. The truth: audiences are mostly indifferent. The few who disagree do so respectfully. The fear is bigger than the reality.

2. The volume needed to grow

I thought I'd need to push hard to grow. Specificity beat volume every time. A small audience of right people compounds faster than a large audience of indifferent people.

3. The need for elaborate visuals

Spent hours on the first few carousel designs. Later carousels with simpler visuals performed equally well. Substance > polish.

4. The importance of optimal posting times

Tested 6 different time slots in May-June. Reach varied <15%. The post itself matters 10x more than when it goes out.

5. The importance of hashtags

Reach with hashtags vs without was within noise. I now use 2-3 minimal ones, mostly for brand discoverability.

6. The career risk of posting opinions publicly

Worried about how my CEO would feel about each post. Reality: she reads them, occasionally comments privately. Has only ever been supportive. The career risk is mostly imaginary.

If you're delaying posting because of any of these — you're overcorrecting. Start.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

Half-Year Recap 13/20 — time spent vs leverage gained.

Honest hours over 6 months:

→ Writing posts: ~150 hours total

→ Engagement: ~100 hours total

→ Planning + Sunday reviews: ~50 hours total

→ Pre-writing for push modes: ~40 hours total

→ DM conversations: ~30 hours

Total: ~370 hours over 6 months. ~14 hours/week average.

What that produced:

→ ~6,600 net new followers

→ ~640 inbound DMs

→ ~31 ICP-qualified buyer DMs

→ 2 deals in active pipeline

→ A network in modern B2B GTM that didn't exist for me before

→ A first-time speaking invite

→ Career optionality I didn't have in May

Hourly rate analysis (rough):

If I value the network alone at 500 hours of equivalent networking effort, this was 1.4x ROI on time.

If I count career optionality, the math is essentially impossible to compute.

Better question than ROI: would I do it again?

Yes. Twice over. The leverage is real. Just slower than people advertise.

#BuildInPublic #CareerJourney

Half-Year Recap 14/20 — the compounding effect, in numbers.

May posts after one month: avg 1,200 impressions each.

May posts after six months: avg 2,800 impressions each.

Same posts. ~2.3x more reach over time. Why?

→ Algorithm surfaces them when new followers join (they read backwards)

→ Search and saves keep them alive

→ Other creators reference them, driving residual traffic

→ I sometimes link back in newer posts, lifting old ones

The 4-Layer Clay Map (May 14) is now the post that has saved me the most onboarding effort with new operators in DMs. I just send the link.

What this means practically:

→ Don't write to perform on day 1. Write to compound over years.

→ Frameworks compound better than POVs (which are timely).

→ Specific tactical posts compound better than abstract advice.

Compounding is invisible week-to-week. Visible quarter-to-quarter.

If you can hold consistency for 12 months, the back half of year 1 starts paying you for the front half.

I'm at the inflection point right now.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

Half-Year Recap 15/20 — cost of consistency, in honest numbers.

What 14 hours/week of content work costs me, weekly:

→ ~3 evenings of personal time (instead of family/friends/gym/reading)

→ Sunday afternoons (planning + drafting)

→ ~1 hour of weekday morning time (engagement)

→ ~30 min of weekday lunch time (post-publish engagement window)

→ Mental load of always thinking 'is this a post?'

Monthly:

→ 1 weekend day per push month (pre-writing)

→ ~1 social event skipped (writing time)

→ ~€60 in tools (Buffer / Notion / occasional design help)

Less measurable:

→ Slight constant background hum of 'should I be writing right now?'

→ Occasional comparisons to creators with bigger numbers

→ Sleep on push-mode Sunday nights (genuine)

Why I keep doing it anyway:

→ The compounding (yesterday's post)

→ The network (Tuesday's post)

→ The clarification of my own thinking

→ The career optionality

→ The fact that the work itself genuinely interests me

If you don't enjoy at least the writing or the engagement, the cost will outpace the benefit. Pick something you'd write about for free.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

*'**d correct*

Half-Year Recap 16/20 — mistakes I'd correct if starting now.

1. I wrote too long for the first month

May posts averaged 240 words. Audience attention drops fast. June onward I tightened to ~140 average. Engagement immediately improved.

2. I didn't have a hook bank ready

First-line hooks made or broke posts. I rewrote some hooks 5+ times. Should have built the bank first, then drafted posts against it.

3. I treated all 5 weekdays the same

Took two months to learn that Tue/Wed/Thu outperform Mon/Fri for my audience. Could have learned this in week 2 by just looking.

4. I delayed carousels for too long

First carousel was Aug. Should have been in May. Carousels are the highest-saves format and would have grown the audience faster.

5. I underused video

Almost no video in 6 months. The 1-2 short videos I posted got disproportionate reach. Wasted opportunity.

6. I overthought push modes

First push felt like a launch event. Second push (this one) felt routine. The first one would have been less stressful with the same playbook.

If you're starting now — fix all 6 in week 1. Save yourself the curve.

#BuildInPublic #LinkedInTips

*'**s coming*

Half-Year Recap 17/20 — what's coming in months 7-12.

December: deepen, not broaden. Slower cadence (3/week). Reflection content. End-of-year predictions. Quality over quantity.

January 2027: the biggest month of year 1. Predictions, year-ahead playbooks, '7 plays I'm betting on for 2027'. Match the proven January pattern in this space.

February: customer & case stories. With permission, real cases — first long-form B2B GTM cases I'll publish.

March: push #3 — conference season. The European business circuit runs deep into spring. Live conference posts + recaps + hot takes.

April: year-one receipts. The big arc — 9k → 25k+. The post becomes the case study.

Beyond month 12: I don't know yet.

Possibilities I'm thinking about:

→ A small podcast for B2B GTM operators

→ A community Slack (operator-focused)

→ More category-build, less personal-build

What I'm certain about: the pace continues. The compounding compounds. And the niche I'm in (modern B2B GTM + marketing automation) gets less invisible every month.

#BuildInPublic #Roadmap

topic: Half-Year Recap 18/20 — advice for someone starting now
body:
Half-Year Recap 18/20 — advice for someone starting now.

The 10 things I wish someone had told me on May 11:

1. Pick a niche you'd write about for free.

2. Build the hook bank before you write the posts.

3. Default to ~120-160 words. Tighten ruthlessly.

4. Carousels from week 1, not month 4.

5. Engagement = 2x more time than writing, for the first 3 months.

6. Sundays are sacred. Plan + draft.

7. Track 5 numbers monthly. Ignore everything else.

8. Reply to every comment within 60 min for the first month.

9. Don't expect inbound business for 3-6 months.

10. Don't quit at month 2. The compounding starts at month 4.

And one more — the most important:

11. Write things that cost you something. Vague posts are safe; specific posts are credible. Real numbers, real mistakes, real opinions. The posts that scared me to publish are the ones that built the account.

Save this if it helps.

#BuildInPublic #LinkedInTips #ContentMarketing

topic: Half-Year Recap 20/20 — the most important lesson
body:
Half-Year Recap 20/20 — the single most important lesson. Last post of the push.

If I had to keep one sentence from six months of posting:

Specificity is the only true differentiator.

Not voice. Not personality. Not aesthetic. Not 'authenticity'.

Specificity.

Real numbers. Real mistakes. Real tools. Real trade-offs. Real customers (with permission). Real failure modes. Real costs. Real time savings. Real reasons you might be wrong.

When you're specific, you can't be replaced by an LLM. You can't be copied by a competitor. You can't be drowned out by louder accounts. The specifics ARE you.

Six months of testing produced this single conviction.

Posts I wrote at general-advice level: middling reach, no compounding.

Posts I wrote at the granular operator level (€40 stack, the 7-line code, the €120k mistake, what 30 operators told me): the ones that built the account.

If you remember nothing else: BE SPECIFIC. Aggressively. Uncomfortably. With numbers and units and dates and tradeoffs.

Push wraps. Recovery week starts Monday.

Onwards to month 7.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

Push #2 wrapped Friday. Numbers:

→ 20 posts, 2 weeks, no missed days

→ Total push-period impressions: ~410k

→ Followers gained during push: 1,920 (target was 1,500)

→ Top post: Half-Year Recap 2/20 (the headline numbers post) — 58k impressions

→ Worst post: Recap 4/20 (the post that flopped) — 9k impressions, ironic because it was about a flop

→ DM inbound: 38 unprompted, 8 from ICP buyers

Difference vs Push #1 (August):

→ Better followers conversion (1,920 vs 1,420)

→ Less burnout (pre-writing 2 weeks ahead vs 4 days)

→ Higher consistency in tone across the 20 posts

→ More inbound, especially in the recovery week (lag effect again)

Recovery week now. Base mode (3 posts). Then back to Growth through Dec 5, then slow down for the December reflection theme.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

*'**ve learned about feedback after 6 months posting*

Six months of posting publicly. What I've learned about feedback:

1. Public feedback is mostly noise.

Likes, reactions, even comment volume — most are reflexive. Useful but not signal.

2. DMs are signal.

When someone DMs me with a specific reaction, that's data. People don't take time to DM unless something landed.

3. Saves are deeper signal.

Saves mean someone wants to come back. They're voting with their bookmarks.

4. Silence on a post you thought was great is data too.

Two of my best-edited posts went silent. Lesson: maybe 'great' was a craft judgement, not a value judgement.

5. The same post lands differently in different weeks.

Timing, context, audience mood matter more than I thought.

6. Negative feedback is rarer and more valuable than positive.

Most people don't take time to disagree. The ones who do are usually right about something.

The biggest mistake I see new creators make: treating likes as feedback. They aren't. They're reflexes.

Track DMs, saves, and the silence on posts you thought were strong.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing #LinkedInTips

Saturday post during recovery. Some honest thoughts after pushes #1 and #2.

Push mode is asymmetric.

Pre-writing 20 posts feels like a sprint.

Posting them feels like a marathon.

Recovery feels like a long warm bath.

What I've learned from doing it twice:

→ The calendar matters more than the content during push. Slipping one day cascades.

→ The pre-writing is 80% of the difficulty. Once written, posting is mechanical.

→ The compounding from push happens in the recovery week, not during the push itself.

→ Two pushes a year would be the right rhythm. Four (per my plan) might be too many. Will recalibrate next year.

Going into December lighter (3/week, Base mode). Slower, deeper, more reflective.

January will be the biggest month of year 1. December is the calm before that.

If you're considering a push mode for the first time — pick a 2-week window 4 weeks from now. Start pre-writing now. Don't try to do both at once.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

Cadence: dropping to 3/week (Base mode). Genuinely.

Why slower in December:

→ My audience scrolls less between Dec 18 and Jan 2

→ Quality > quantity for end-of-year content

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

Year-end is a forcing function for stack audits. Doing mine this week.

Audit checklist (annual, not quarterly):

→ TOOLS: list every paid tool. For each: are we using >50% of what we pay for? If not, downgrade or cancel.

→ INTEGRATIONS: which integrations do we use weekly? Anything monthly or less = candidate for removal.

→ DATA: where does customer data live? Is it consistent? Is anyone copying between systems manually?

→ AUTOMATIONS: any flow that hasn't fired in 30+ days. Archive.

→ SECURITY: who has access to what? Especially important for departing team members.

→ CONTRACTS: when do contracts renew? Negotiate before auto-renewal, not after.

What I expect to find this year:

→ At least 2 tools we'll cancel

→ At least 1 integration that's silently broken

→ At least 5 stale automations

→ Probably 1 access we should have removed months ago

Time investment: ~1 day for the full audit. ROI: usually 5-10x in saved tool costs alone, before counting clean-data benefits.

#GTM #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: What working with engineers taught me
body:
Three years of working closely with engineers as a marketer. The lessons that transferred:

1. SHIP THE 'UGLY VERSION' EARLY

Engineers ship MVPs because polishing too early hides design flaws. Marketers should too. The first draft of any campaign should be deliberately rough.

2. WRITE IT DOWN IN ONE PLACE

Engineers obsess over single sources of truth. Marketing teams that fight over 'which doc is current' lose hours per week. Pick one home for each artefact and defend it.

3. POSTMORTEMS WITHOUT BLAME

Every engineering team I've watched runs blameless postmortems. Marketing teams almost never do. Failed campaigns are taught by the calendar, not by the team.

4. NAMING MATTERS

Engineers spend disproportionate time on variable names. They're right. A campaign named 'Q3-EMEA-NEW-GO-TO-MARKET' lives forever. Naming is design.

5. INSTRUMENT BEFORE YOU OPTIMISE

Engineers measure before they tune. Marketers often optimise emotion-first. Wrong order. Add tracking, then change copy.

If you're a B2B marketer — find an engineer to lunch with monthly. The transfer of mental models is worth more than any conference.

#CareerJourney #MarketingOps #Engineering

*'**AI agents in B2B**'** will actually look like*

Everyone's talking about AI agents in B2B GTM. Here's what I think will actually ship in 2027:

WHAT WON'T HAPPEN:

→ Fully autonomous SDRs (still need human review on outbound that touches buyers)

→ AI-replacing-marketing-teams (replaced ones lacked specialists, the gap is human, not technical)

→ End-to-end campaign agents that 'just work' (orchestration is still hard)

WHAT WILL:

→ Research agents that read 10x more than any human can, summarised on demand

→ Single-purpose tactical agents (e.g. 'monitor this account and alert on signal X')

→ Workflow assistants that propose next steps in CRM, not execute them autonomously

→ 'Co-pilot' style assistance in existing tools (Clay, HubSpot, Apollo) rather than new standalone products

Why the skeptical take:

B2B sales is high-stakes. Buyers don't want to be sold to by agents. The win zone is augmenting humans, not replacing.

The teams that win 2027: the ones that wire research agents in well, while keeping humans in the buyer-facing loop.

Save this. Revisit in December 2027.

#AI #GTM #B2BMarketing

Why year-end B2B content tends to be the best content of the year.

December posts on LinkedIn outperform other months for me by ~25% on engagement, even at lower frequency.

I think it's because:

→ Audience reads more reflectively (less skim, more save)

→ Operators are mentally closing chapters and opening new ones — receptive to lessons-based content

→ Less competition for feed attention from new product launches and event announcements

→ Audiences value honesty more in December than in May

What works in year-end content:

→ Real numbers from your year (yours, not industry reports)

→ Clear lessons stated directly

→ Predictions where you commit (not 'X will be important')

→ Acknowledgement of what didn't work

→ A single sentence of intent for next year

What doesn't:

→ 'Year-in-review' posts that are thinly-veiled self-promotion

→ 'Top 10 trends' posts that say everything that's already obvious

→ Generic gratitude posts with no specifics

December is the most generous month for honest, specific content. Use it.

#ContentMarketing #B2BMarketing

*'**d give my Cairo-self*

The career advice I'd give my Cairo-self three years ago, knowing what I know now:

1. The boring job is teaching you skills the next job will need.

I dismissed translation work as 'just translation'. It taught me sentence-level discipline that nobody else on my marketing teams has. Don't dismiss boring jobs.

2. Pick a tool, learn it deeply, the rest follows.

I picked n8n in week 2 of marketing. Three years later that single deep skill opened more doors than any general marketing knowledge.

3. Move countries before you feel ready.

Waiting for fluency, savings, perfect job is waiting forever. Move first. The fluency comes from the move.

4. Public work is its own internship.

Posting on LinkedIn for free taught me more about marketing in 6 months than my first 18 months of paid work did.

5. Imposter syndrome doesn't fade — but it stops mattering.

Three languages, two countries, two career pivots in. Still feel like an imposter every Monday. Just don't let it stop the work.

6. The career path you'll have isn't named yet.

I describe what I do differently every 6 months. Marketing automation specialist isn't a final answer. It's a step.

If you're earlier than me — none of this is wisdom. It's just data from one path.

#CareerJourney #LessonsLearned

A practical post during the slow month. End-of-year HubSpot hygiene checklist.

Do these before December 22:

1. CLOSE-LOST CLEANUP

Every deal in 'closed-lost' from this year — does it have a close-reason populated? If not, fix. This is the data your 2027 ICP refresh runs on.

2. STALE OPEN DEALS

Any deal in 'open' that hasn't had activity in 90+ days — close it (lost-no-decision) or move it to 'paused'. Don't let it ride into 2027.

3. CONTACT OWNERSHIP

Departing team members' contacts get reassigned. Most teams forget. The person who left in October still has 600 contacts under their name.

4. PIPELINE STAGES

Are your stages still describing reality? If a stage is barely used, archive. If a stage represents two different things, split.

5. PROPERTY USAGE

Run a properties-not-touched-in-90-days report. Archive what you don't use.

6. INACTIVE WORKFLOWS

Any workflow paused or unused for 90+ days — archive. Active stale workflows are worse than no workflow.

Time: ~half a day total.

Result: Q1 2027 starts on cleaner data, and your forecasts are more trustworthy.

#HubSpot #RevOps #MarketingOps

topic: 5 things I was wrong about in 2026
body:
5 things I was wrong about in 2026:

1. I thought volume was the lever for growth.

It's specificity. A 100-word post with one specific number outperforms a 400-word general-advice post every time.

2. I thought engagement gimmicks would work.

Engagement pods, agree/disagree bait, tagging large creators. None of it. Real conversation works. Gimmicks decay in weeks.

3. I thought 'AI-written outbound at scale' was the future.

It was already over by the time I tested it. AI-RESEARCHED outbound is the future. Different tool, same letters.

4. I thought our ICP was clear.

It was clear in our heads. Not in our data. Refreshing the ICP in August surfaced 80 contacts we should have prioritised months earlier.

5. I thought I'd peak at month 3.

Most growth happened months 4-6. The compounding is real. The patience is the unlock.

Being publicly wrong is uncomfortable. It's also the cheapest way to update your map of the world.

I'll be wrong about more things in 2027. Looking forward to writing the same post in December 2027.

#BuildInPublic #ContentMarketing

2027 predictions. Carousel format. (10 slides, first in the comments.)

B2B GTM — six predictions:

1. Outbound volume keeps shrinking. Reply rates on small targeted lists keep climbing.

2. Clay (or its successor) becomes mid-market default in B2B by Q4 2027.

3. Multilingual cultural-rewrite (not translation) becomes a real category.

4. AI agents in GTM remain mostly research, not execution. Agents that act autonomously stay rare.

5. CRM-as-system-of-record holds. CRM-as-cleverness loses to specialised tools.

If you're an operator reading this in the gap-week — use it for one thing:

Make a list of every tool, system, workflow, and decision you'd KEEP from 2026. Not what you'd change. What you'd keep.

Most year-end planning is about what to change. The harder, more useful exercise is naming what's working — so you don't accidentally break it in next year's redesign.
